x: math for interaction design — roadmap start here — the plain idea think of this whole course as learning one new pair of glasses. right now, when you see a slick animation, you probably see moves — a card slides, a wave wobbles, cells tilt. put these glasses on and you start seeing functions : a number going in a finger, a clock, an item's index and a number coming out where, how big, how tilted . the magic word for this course is "model": turn the picture into a little math machine, then turn its knobs. the payoff is that you stop guessing. instead of nudging "0.45" until it feels right, you describe the relationship once and everything downstream follows. the flagship example here is a wavy carousel where cells sit on a sine wave and tilt to match it — that one screen secretly uses a wave, a derivative for the tilt , and an integral for even spacing . learn those few moves and you can place and aim anything on any path. this roadmap is the map. each lesson is one tool in the kit; the order builds from "what's a function" up to the full carousel and then to how ios actually computes these values frame by frame. the skill isn't "advanced math." it's a way of seeing : model the visual as a function, then use calculus / linear algebra to extract the geometric thing you need. this curriculum exists because of one caption on a wavy carousel: "calculating the derivative of that wave

01 — modeling visuals as functions start here — the plain idea a function is just a vending machine: you feed it something a finger position, a clock, an item number and it hands back something a height, a tilt, an opacity . modeling a visual "as a function" means refusing to think in vague moves — "it kind of slides up" — and instead naming the exact machine: in goes , out comes . why bother? because once it's a machine, calculus is just a set of buttons on the side of it. the derivative button tells you which way the curve is leaning right there — that's your rotation. the integral button adds up tiny steps along the path — that's your distance, for spacing things evenly. you don't compute these by hand; you just need to know what each button gives you geometrically. the picture to hold: imagine driving a car along a curvy road. where you are is the position. the way your nose points is the tangent the 1st derivative . how hard you're cranking the wheel is the curvature the 2nd . and your odometer — total distance driven — is the integral. same car, four different readouts. that's the entire lesson. the single habit that separates "i tweak values until it looks right" from "i can derive exactly what i need." read this first — straight lines vs curves. your school intuition — "acceleration = how fast speed changes" — is the 1d / straight line picture. on a line there's only on

01a — choosing the math a decision recipe start here — the plain idea this lesson is a sorting hat. you describe your interaction in one plain sentence — "as x happens, y changes" — and a couple of yes/no questions sort it into the right pile of math. you're not memorizing formulas; you're learning which drawer to open. the trick that makes this work: almost every interaction's input is one of a tiny set time, a gesture, an index, a distance and its output is one of a tiny set position, scale, rotation, opacity, color . so really you're matching one small list to another, and the fork "does it map, cycle, or simulate?" decides the rest. the hardest part isn't the formulas — it's knowing which one to reach for. the secret: experienced people don't recall the right formula, they answer a few plain questions and the formula falls out. here's that process, the way you'd build a formula from nothing. if you can't write the interaction as one sentence, you don't have an interaction yet — you have a vibe. start with the sentence. step 1 — write it as one sentence: "as input changes, output changes" force yourself to name both ends, with ranges : "as the finger drags down input: 0 → 300 pt , the card shrinks output: scale 1.0 → 0.5 ." that sentence is the spec. in practice the input is almost always one of: time a clock ticking a gesture / scroll position drag offset, contentoffset an

01b — the seven building blocks start here — the plain idea think of these seven as the lego bricks of motion. almost nothing in ui is built from scratch — it's these few tiny functions snapped together. most are about blending between two values ; a couple are about cycling forever ; one is a mini physics toy . the one to fall in love with is : "as this slides from a to b, make that slide from c to d." two rulers taped together — point at the top ruler, read straight down to the bottom. master that one and you've covered most of what you'll ever wire up. almost every interaction is built from the same handful of tiny functions. learn these seven and you can express most of what you'll ever need. each one below is given once , with everything in one place: a plain english idea, the formula, a mental picture, runnable swiftui code, and a number trace so you can see it. if you remember only one thing from this whole topic, make it : "as this goes from a to b, make that go from c to d." that single idea covers most interactions. first, paste these helpers once — swift doesn't ship / / : how to study each one: paste the snippet into an xcode preview, then move the slider slowly while picturing the image — that's the moment the formula clicks. 1. — mix two values the dimmer slider idea: give it a start, an end, and a percent → it returns the point that % of the way between them. pic

02 — trigonometry & the unit circle start here — the plain idea forget triangles for a second. picture a dot walking around a circle at steady pace. watch only its height over time and you get a smooth up down wiggle — that wiggle is . watch only its sideways position and you get the same wiggle, just shifted — that's . so a wave isn't some new thing; it's circular motion seen edge on. that's why one idea unlocks three effects at once. anything that breathes, pulses, or repeats forever is the height of that walking dot . anything that goes around — a radial menu, a loading spinner — is the dot's full position. and rotating something is just nudging where the dot started on the circle. the three knobs on a wave are easy to feel: amplitude is how tall the wiggle is, frequency is how fast the dot walks how many wiggles , and phase is where on the circle it began slides the whole wave sideways . pinch to stretch on the carousel? that's just turning the amplitude knob. not "soh cah toa." the one idea that unlocks waves, rotation, and oscillation at once. the core insight and are the x and y of a point travelling around the unit circle. so a wave is circular motion projected onto a line. oscillation, rotation, and waves are the same phenomenon seen from different angles. once you see this, half of "creative math" collapses into one picture. anatomy of a wave amplitude — how tall. pin

03 — calculus: the derivative as tangent start here — the plain idea the derivative sounds scary; geometrically it's just "the way the curve is leaning right here." stand at any point on a hill and lay a ruler flat against the slope — the tilt of that ruler is the derivative. and a tilt is a direction, which is exactly what you need to rotate a cell so it sits flush on the curve. that's the whole carousel trick in one sentence. three buttons, three pictures. the first derivative is that leaning ruler — which way it faces . the second derivative is how fast the lean is changing — how hard the curve bends use it to squash things on tight corners . the integral is the odd one out: instead of breaking things down, it adds tiny pieces back up, like an odometer ticking off distance as you drive — that's how you measure length along a curve so you can space items evenly. so don't picture algebra. picture a tangent line riding along a curve as you drag a point: its angle is your rotation, how quickly that angle swings is your curvature, and the running total of distance is your arc length. you don't need symbol pushing. you need the geometric meaning of three operations. derivative = slope = tangent direction the derivative is the slope of the curve at . slope is a direction. that's why it gives you rotation. the six derivatives worth memorizing chain rule is why — the inner pops out.

04 — vectors & linear algebra start here — the plain idea a vector is just an arrow: it has a direction and a length, and that's it. the moment you stop juggling separate and and start thinking "arrow," your code shrinks and the geometry gets obvious. position, velocity, an offset, the direction a cell faces — all arrows. two little operations do most of the heavy lifting. the dot product answers "how much do these two arrows point the same way?" — big positive means aligned, zero means perpendicular, negative means opposing. think "is the user dragging toward this thing?" the cross product in 2d, a single signed number answers "which way does the turn go — left or right?" — that's how you tell which side of a line you're on. one handy reflex: to get the perpendicular of an arrow , just swap and flip a sign → . that's a 90° turn, and it's how you push a cell off to the side of a curve along its normal. and rotation? it's a tiny 2×2 grid of / numbers — the same trig from the last lesson, wearing a matrix costume. the native language of graphics. two products do most of the work. a vector is a direction with length position, velocity, normal, offset — all vectors. learn to think in vectors instead of separate / and the code shrinks. dot product = "how aligned are these two directions?" → same general direction · → perpendicular · → opposing. uses: lighting , "is the user dragging

05 — parametric curves & splines start here — the plain idea so far a path was "height as you move right" . but that can't draw a loop, a circle, or a vertical line — the path would have to be in two places at once. the fix is to stop describing the shape and start describing the trip : let one dial run from 0 to 1, and at each moment say where the dot is, . now the dot can loop, cross itself, do anything — like an etch a sketch driven by time. the bonus is huge: the same derivative trick still works. the dot's velocity — how and are changing right now — points straight along the path. that's your tangent, on any curve, for free. béziers are the everyday version: four points where two are endpoints and two are "handles" you tug to bend the curve, exactly like the pen tool in design apps. catmull rom is the cousin that politely passes through every point you give it — perfect for "lay these cells smoothly along these spots." to place things evenly, you walk the curve in tiny steps, total up the length, and drop items at equal distances — the carousel recipe, now on any path. generalize "the wave" to any path — circles, arcs, hand drawn strokes, béziers. parametric form: describe motion, not just shape instead of , use a parameter : this handles loops and verticals that can't, and the tangent falls right out of the derivative — same trick as 03 derivative as tangent, now on any p

06 — interpolation, easing & springs start here — the plain idea this is the workhorse lesson — the math behind most everyday ui. the core idea is blending: slides between two values, and a 0→1 dial is how far along you are. stack a few of these and you get : "as the scroll goes from a to b, make the header scale go from c to d." that single sentence is most of interaction design. is wearing nicer shoes — instead of a robotic constant pace, it eases away gently, speeds up, and eases to a stop, so transitions don't snap. that gentle s curve is what "easeinout" really is. springs are a different animal. instead of drawing a fixed path, you hand physics a target and let it settle there — like releasing a screen door. it rushes over, maybe overshoots and wobbles, then comes to rest. the beauty is you can change the target mid flight great for gestures , and "critically damped" just means "tuned to arrive fast with no bounce." use blending when you know both endpoints; use a spring when something needs to feel physical and interruptible. the small toolkit you reach for in every falloff — ui or shader. deep companion: interpolation and easing. the five functions to know cold + are 80% of "make this value drive that effect smoothly." the stories strip edge falloff was exactly over a remapped distance. easing = reshaping before you lerp an easing curve is just a function applied to pro

06a — simulations beyond the spring start here — the plain idea the spring taught you a powerful move: instead of drawing a motion curve, you give a tiny simulation a rule and let it generate the motion . the spring is just one member of a whole family that works this way — and once you have a few of them, you can make almost anything feel physical. they all share the exact same shape. you keep a little bit of state — usually a , sometimes a too — and every frame you update it from a small formula. because the value reacts to a rule instead of following a script, the path emerges , and emergent paths are what read as "natural." the spring's was one such rule; here are the other rules worth owning. think of this as your physics cabinet : reach for the one whose feel matches what you want glide, bounce, snap, drift , tune one or two knobs by hand, and let the frame loop do the rest. every formula below runs inside a per frame loop a in swiftui, or any 60–120 fps tick . is the seconds since the last frame — it keeps the motion the same real world speed on any display. see 09 what drives the value. 1. exponential smoothing — the "chase" no velocity, so it can never overshoot — it just eases toward a target and slows as it arrives. feel: calm ease out, always settles gently. use: smoothing a jittery sensor or cursor, easing a number counter, a soft follow. it's a spring with the bou

07 — oscillations, noise & organic motion start here — the plain idea a plain wave wiggles forever at the same size — fine for a steady pulse, but real things lose energy. multiply the wave by a fading curtain , a shrinking envelope and the wiggle dies down over time — that's a bounce settling, the natural cousin of a spring. two more tricks make motion feel alive instead of looping. first: stack a few sine waves at speeds that don't line up neatly, and the result never visibly repeats — it just drifts, gently, forever. pick "ugly" numbers like 0.6 and 0.43 so they never sync. second: noise. regular jitters like static; perlin/simplex noise is smooth random — ask it about two nearby moments and it gives you two nearby answers. that smoothness is exactly what reads as organic — smoke, a flag, a hand's slight tremor. the pro recipe layers these: a resting position, plus slow summed sine breathing, plus a touch of noise to wander, plus a spring so it still reacts to your touch. boring parts, alive result. how to make motion feel alive instead of mechanical. damped oscillation = bounce that dies out the envelope is the secret to natural settling — it's the analytic cousin of the spring in 06 interpolation easing and springs. see damped harmonic oscillator. summed sines fourier intuition any periodic motion is a sum of sine waves. layer a few sines at different frequencies/amplitude

t: 09 — What Drives the Value (the iOS Bridge)
x: 09 — what drives the value the ios bridge start here — the plain idea the rest of the course told you what number you want — the height, the tilt, the opacity. this lesson asks a different question: who keeps recomputing that number, and when? same effect can be wired three very different ways, and picking the right one is what makes an interaction feel clean instead of janky. three drivers, in plain terms. first, input driven: the value is just a function of where your finger or scroll is right now — no animation at all, it's literally . the wavy carousel and stories edge scaling live here, which is why they feel glued to your finger. second, a tween: you name a start and end and let the system smoothly blend between them — a button press, a sheet opening. third, per frame: you recompute every single frame in a loop, needed for ongoing simulations like particles or physics fields. the discipline is to name three things before you code: what function gives the value, which driver owns it, and which layer core animation / metal actually draws it. get those three and the implementation is nearly mechanical. the math tells you what the value should be. this note tells you how ios should compute it over time. same three categories, every interaction. the three drivers driver the value is… ios tools example input driven a pure function or — no animation , , , gesture translation sto

10 — study method start here — the plain idea math like this doesn't stick from reading — it sticks from fiddling . the single most important habit on this page: before you write a line of code, build the interaction as a function in desmos and drag the sliders. watching the tangent swing as you move a point teaches your gut something no paragraph can. the loop is simple: spot an interaction you love, guess its function and driver, rebuild it in desmos with sliders, derive whatever it needs tangent, normal, curvature , then build it for real and compare. ten honest reps of that beat any course. treat your shader playground as a lab — it already speaks , vectors, and oscillation, so new concepts have somewhere to land immediately. the anti pattern to avoid: jumping straight to code and tuning magic numbers, instead of modeling first and deriving the relationship. the method matters more than the reading list. four habits. 1. desmos first, code second before writing any code, model the interaction as a function in desmos with sliders. take its derivative there; watch the tangent move. the interactivity builds intuition that reading can't. this is the most important habit on the page. 2. drill the meta move pick a curve wave, circle, spiral, bézier and extract its tangent orientation , normal perpendicular , and curvature bend . do it for five different curves until it's automatic

t: 01 — Why Swift: History, Safety, and the Ecosystem
x: 01 — why swift: history, safety, and the ecosystem swift is the language apple created to replace objective c. understanding why it exists helps you understand why its features work the way they do. a brief history the key insight: swift was designed from scratch to fix objective c's pain points — messaging, manual memory management, c header files, pointer arithmetic — while keeping interoperability with existing apple frameworks. safety: swift's core philosophy swift's defining characteristic is safety by default . the compiler catches entire categories of bugs at compile time. optional safety — no more null pointer crashes this single feature eliminates the most common crash in software: the null pointer dereference. you'll explore optionals deeply in 08 optionals 08 optionals.md . type safety — no implicit conversions memory safety — arc does the work swift uses automatic reference counting arc to manage memory. no / , no garbage collector pauses. covered in depth in 18 memory management 18 memory management.md . performance swift is a compiled language. it's not interpreted like python or javascript — your code is compiled to native machine code. key performance features: value types — structs live on the stack, no heap allocation overhead copy on write — large collections only copy when modified whole module optimization — the compiler inlines and optimizes across files z

02 — variables and types every swift program starts with data. this lesson covers how to declare variables, what types exist, and how swift's type system keeps you safe. constant vs variable rule of thumb : use by default. switch to only when you need to mutate the value. xcode will warn you if you use but never mutate it. why this matters: immutability makes code easier to reason about. when you see , you know that value never changes. this becomes critical in concurrent code see 20 concurrency basics 20 concurrency basics.md . type inference vs explicit annotation swift can figure out types from context. you don't need to write them — but you can. convention : omit type annotations when the type is obvious from the right side. add them when it's ambiguous or when you want a different type than the default. basic types integer types use for almost everything. use sized types only when interfacing with specific apis or binary formats. floating point types use by default. use when an api requires it like some graphics apis . boolean string and character strings are covered in depth in 03 strings 03 strings.md . type conversion — no implicit conversion swift never converts types automatically. this prevents subtle bugs. the returning an optional is your first taste of optionals — see 08 optionals 08 optionals.md for the full story. tuples tuples group multiple values into a singl

03 — strings strings in swift are powerful, unicode correct, and safer than in most languages. the tradeoff is that string indexing works differently than you might expect. strings are value types a in swift is a struct, not a class. when you assign or pass a string, it copies though swift optimizes this with copy on write internally — see 19 value semantics 19 value semantics.md . string interpolation embed expressions inside strings with . this is the preferred way to build strings in swift. avoid string concatenation for building complex strings. interpolation is faster and more readable. multiline strings use triple quotes for strings that span multiple lines. special characters raw strings when you have lots of special characters, wrap the string in to disable escape sequences. unicode: swift strings are unicode correct every swift is a collection of values, where each is a unicode extended grapheme cluster — what a human perceives as a single character. this is why swift strings aren't integer indexable. character type string.index — not int! this is the most surprising part of swift strings for newcomers. you cannot index a string with an integer. substrings substring operations return a type, which shares storage with the original string for efficiency. convert to if you need to store it long term. common string operations string comparison building strings see learning

06 — functions functions are the building blocks of swift programs. swift functions have a distinctive feature: argument labels that make call sites read like prose. basic syntax the anatomy: no return value single expression functions when the body is a single expression, is implicit. argument labels and parameter names swift functions can have two names for each parameter: an argument label used at the call site and a parameter name used inside the function . omitting argument labels use to omit the label. why argument labels? labels make code self documenting: default parameter values parameters with defaults can be omitted at the call site. place parameters with defaults at the end. variadic parameters accept zero or more values of a specific type. a function can have multiple variadic parameters swift 5.4+ , but the parameter after a variadic one must have a label. inout parameters — pass by reference by default, function parameters are constants . use to modify the caller's variable. is how swiftui's works under the hood. when you pass to a child view, you're essentially passing an inout reference. see learning swiftui/05 state and binding ../learning swiftui/05 state and binding.md for how uses this concept. functions as first class citizens functions can be assigned to variables, stored in collections, and passed as arguments. function types every function has a type, d

closures start here — the plain idea a closure is just a chunk of code you can hand around like a value. think of it as a function you can put in a variable, pass as an argument, or return from another function. you already use them constantly — every you pass to or is a closure. the "closure" part of the name is the interesting bit: when you write that chunk of code, it quietly grabs onto the variables around it and keeps them alive. it's like a backpack — the code zips up the surrounding variables it needs and carries them wherever it goes, even after the function that created it has returned. that's why a counter closure can keep counting: the variable is in its backpack. most of this lesson is about syntax shortcuts swift loves to let you drop words until is all that's left and about controlling that backpack — because if a closure grabs , and is also holding the closure, neither can ever be freed. that's the retain cycle that exists to break. closures are self contained blocks of functionality that can be passed around and used in your code. they capture and store references to variables and constants from the surrounding context. functions are actually a special case of closures every function you have written so far is a closure with a name. full closure syntax the complete form of a closure includes parameter types, return type, and the keyword to separate the header fr

optionals swift uses optionals to handle the absence of a value. unlike languages that use or implicitly, swift makes the possibility of "no value" explicit in the type system. this eliminates an entire class of bugs at compile time rather than at runtime. what is nil? in many languages, any reference can be , and accessing it causes a crash. swift takes a different approach: a regular variable can never be nil. if a value might be absent, you must declare it as an optional type. the compiler then forces you to handle the nil case before using the value. optional is an enum under the hood, is just a generic enum with two cases: writing is syntactic sugar for . you can even use the long form: understanding this enum structure makes every optional operation intuitive you are always just pattern matching on two cases. declaring optionals the distinction between and matters. the first is "maybe an array," the second is "definitely an array, but individual elements may be nil." force unwrapping the operator extracts the value from an optional. if the optional is nil, your program crashes. rule of thumb: avoid force unwrapping. it defeats the purpose of optionals. use it only when you can prove the value exists and a nil would indicate a programmer error. optional binding with if let the safe way to unwrap. the unwrapped value exists only inside the braces. shorthand syntax swift 5.7

enums enumerations in swift are far more powerful than in most languages. they are first class types that can have associated values, raw values, computed properties, methods, initializers, and protocol conformances. enums are value types, like structs. basic enum declaration an enum defines a type with a fixed set of related values. enums shine when you have a closed set of possibilities. the compiler knows all cases, which enables exhaustive statements. because the switch is exhaustive, no case is needed. if you add a new case to the enum later, the compiler will flag every switch that does not handle it. associated values each case can carry different types of data. this is what makes swift enums so expressive. associated values make enums perfect for modeling states, api responses, and domain events. raw values enums can have a raw type. each case gets a fixed value of that type. string raw values default to the case name: important: an enum can have either raw values or associated values, not both. caseiterable conform to to get an automatically generated collection of all cases. this is useful for building menus, pickers, and settings screens. note that only works automatically when no case has associated values. methods and computed properties enums can have methods and computed properties just like structs. mutating methods since enums are value types, methods that chan

structs vs classes swift gives you two primary ways to define custom types with properties and methods: structs and classes. the fundamental difference is that structs are value types copied on assignment and classes are reference types shared on assignment . this distinction affects everything from memory behavior to thread safety to api design. structs: value types when you assign a struct to a new variable or pass it to a function, swift copies the data. each variable holds its own independent copy. structs get an automatic memberwise initializer based on their stored properties. you do not need to write unless you want custom behavior. classes: reference types when you assign a class instance to a new variable, both variables point to the same object in memory. notice that even though is declared with , its properties can change because only prevents reassigning the reference, not mutating the object it points to. the identity test: === vs == checks if two values are equal same content . checks if two references point to the same object in memory. structs do not support because they do not have identity. they are just values. struct advantages thread safety since each copy is independent, structs can be used across threads without synchronization. predictability no shared mutable state means no spooky action at a distance. when you pass a struct to a function, you know it c

properties properties associate values with a type. swift has several kinds of properties, each serving a different purpose: stored properties hold data, computed properties calculate values on demand, and property observers react to changes. mastering properties is essential because they form the backbone of every type you write. stored properties the simplest kind. a stored property is a constant or variable that is part of an instance. if the struct instance itself is , none of its properties can be modified: for classes, on the instance only prevents reassigning the reference. properties marked can still change. lazy stored properties a is not calculated until it is first accessed. use this for expensive initialization that may not always be needed. rules for lazy properties: must be the first access mutates the struct to store the value cannot be not thread safe by default the instance must be for structs since accessing a lazy property mutates it computed properties a computed property does not store a value. it provides a getter and optionally a setter that calculates a value each time. get only computed property: get/set computed property: the keyword is the implicit name for the incoming value in a setter. you can also name it explicitly: . property observers property observers respond to changes in a stored property's value. they are called every time the value is set

protocols protocols define a blueprint of methods, properties, and other requirements that a type must fulfill. they are the foundation of swift's protocol oriented programming paradigm. where other languages lean on inheritance hierarchies, swift encourages composition through protocols. protocol declaration a protocol specifies what conforming types must provide. it declares required properties with or and methods without bodies . properties in a protocol use for read only or for read write. the conforming type can implement with a stored property, a computed property, or even a constant. methods in a protocol have no body conforming types supply the implementation. protocol conformance a type conforms to a protocol by implementing all its requirements. you can also add conformance in an extension, which is useful for separating concerns or conforming types you do not own: protocol inheritance protocols can inherit from other protocols, building up requirements. a type conforming to must satisfy all requirements from , , and . protocol composition combine multiple protocols into a single requirement using . this is more flexible than protocol inheritance because the combination is defined at the use site, not in the protocol hierarchy. you can also combine a protocol with a class type: protocol extensions: default implementations protocol extensions let you provide default be

error handling start here — the plain idea in swift, an error isn't some magical event that blows up your program — it's just a value. you define what can go wrong usually an enum: , , and when it happens, you that value. the compiler then forces whoever calls you to deal with it. no silent failures, no surprise crashes deep in the call stack. the labels work just like a warning sticker on a function. = "heads up, this might fail." when you call something that throws, you write in front — that's you acknowledging "yes, i know this could go wrong." and is the safety net: run the risky code in the , and if anything throws, jump to a to handle it. think of as the word that lets a thrown error travel up to the nearest . there are softer variants for when you don't need the full ceremony: means "if it fails, just give me and forget the details," and means "i promise this won't fail — crash if i'm wrong." the rest of the lesson is about richer tools: an error or success stored as a value to deal with later , nicer user facing messages, and swift 6's typed throws that let you say exactly which error a function can throw. swift treats errors as values that can be thrown, caught, and transformed. unlike exceptions in many languages, swift errors don't unwind the call stack unpredictably the compiler forces you to handle every possible failure path explicitly. see 09 enums ../learning sw

access control access control restricts which parts of your code can access other parts. it lets you hide implementation details and expose a clean public interface. swift has five access levels, giving you fine grained control over visibility at the module and file level. the five access levels from most restrictive to least restrictive: level visibility only within the enclosing declaration type or extension in same file anywhere within the same source file anywhere within the same module default accessible from other modules, but can't subclass or override accessible from other modules, can subclass and override default is internal if you don't specify an access level, swift defaults to . this means everything in your module can access it, but nothing outside can: for single module apps most ios apps , is effectively "visible everywhere." access control becomes critical when you write frameworks, swift packages, or share code across targets. private vs fileprivate restricts access to the enclosing declaration and extensions of that type in the same file: allows access from anywhere in the same source file: rule of thumb: use by default. use only when multiple types in the same file need to share implementation details. public vs open both are visible outside the module. the difference is about inheritance and overriding : is the highest access level. use it when you design a

memory management swift uses automatic reference counting arc to manage memory for class instances. arc is deterministic objects are deallocated the instant their reference count hits zero. understanding arc is essential because reference cycles can cause memory leaks that silently degrade your app. see 07 closures ../learning swift/07 closures.md for closure capture semantics and 10 structs vs classes ../learning swift/10 structs vs classes.md for why this only applies to reference types. how arc works every class instance has a reference count a hidden integer tracking how many strong references point to it. arc increments the count when a new reference is created and decrements it when a reference goes away. when the count reaches zero, arc deallocates the instance. this is deterministic unlike garbage collection, you know exactly when runs. strong reference cycles a cycle occurs when two objects hold strong references to each other. neither can reach zero, and both leak: weak references a reference does not increment the reference count. it automatically becomes when the referenced object is deallocated. must be and optional: use when the referenced object may legitimately outlive or be deallocated before the reference holder. unowned references an reference also doesn't increment the count, but it does not become nil. accessing it after the object is deallocated crashes :

property wrappers start here — the plain idea a property wrapper is a way to attach reusable behavior to a stored property. you've probably written the same getter/setter logic in three different places — trim this string, clamp this number to a range, save this value to userdefaults. a property wrapper lets you bottle that logic once and apply it with a single attribute, like . behind the scenes, swift quietly stores a wrapper instance and routes every read and write of through its . think of it as a sleeve that slips around your property. from the outside looks like a normal , but every time you set it, the sleeve runs its logic first. this is exactly the machinery behind swiftui's and — they're just property wrappers doing bookkeeping each time the value changes. two extras round it out. the prefix exposes a wrapper's — a second, related value the wrapper hands you alongside the main one like "is this valid?" . and key paths are a separate but kindred idea: a type safe, storable reference to a property — "the name field" as a value you can pass around, hand to , or use to forward access. property wrappers define reusable logic for property access. they are the mechanism behind swiftui's , , and . this chapter also covers key paths and dynamic member lookup. see properties ../learning swift/11 properties.md for computed properties and observers that wrappers build on. see gen

swift internals start here — the plain idea this lesson is the "open the hood and look at the engine" chapter. everything you've written so far runs on machinery the compiler and runtime quietly manage — this is where you finally see the gears. a few big questions drive it. when you call a method, how does swift know which code to actually run? that's dispatch: sometimes the compiler knows the exact function ahead of time and can hard wire the call static dispatch — fast, inlinable ; other times it has to look the answer up in a table at runtime a vtable for class overrides, a witness table for protocol calls . faster when known, more flexible when looked up — that's the whole trade off. the cost of vs from the generics chapter? this is where it comes from: wraps your value in a 40 byte box and dispatches through a table; stays concrete and gets inlined. the rest is the supporting cast. type metadata is the runtime's "spec sheet" for every type its size, alignment, how to copy it . abi stability is the promise that compiled swift binaries can talk to each other across compiler versions — which is why your app borrows the os's swift runtime instead of shipping its own. and result builders are the trick that turns a plain block of statements into a single value — the compiler rewrites into builder calls behind your back. that rewrite is exactly how swiftui's works. under the hood

swiftui — learning roadmap start here — the plain idea think of this page as the table of contents for a long road trip. you're not learning anything new here — you're just looking at the map: where you'll start, what you'll pass through, and roughly how long each leg takes. the "prerequisites" list is basically the packing checklist bring your swift knowledge , and the part by part tables are the route. swiftui itself is apple's way of building app screens by describing what you want instead of assembling it piece by piece. the big idea — the one every lesson keeps circling back to — is that your ui is a function of your data: change the data, and the screen redraws itself. you don't have to remember to update labels by hand. everything downstream follows from that one shift, which is why the roadmap front loads philosophy and state. a self paced curriculum from your first to building production swiftui apps. 6 parts, 25 lessons. prerequisites complete learning swift ../learning swift/00 roadmap.md or at minimum lessons 01 14 through generics . swiftui leans heavily on: structs lesson 10 — every view is a struct protocols lesson 13 — the protocol is foundational closures lesson 07 — swiftui uses trailing closures everywhere generics lesson 14 — is an opaque return type property wrappers lesson 24 — , , if you haven't finished lessons 15 25 yet, you can start here and return to

t: 01 — SwiftUI Philosophy: Declarative UI and the View Protocol
x: 01 — swiftui philosophy: declarative ui and the view protocol start here — the plain idea imagine ordering a coffee. the old way imperative / uikit is like walking behind the counter yourself: grind the beans, pull the shot, steam the milk, pour. you give step by step instructions and you do the work. the new way declarative / swiftui is like telling the barista "a flat white" — you describe the result you want, and someone else figures out the steps. that's the whole shift. in swiftui you write a description of what the screen should look like for the current data, and the framework handles the how. when the data changes, you don't reach in and tweak a label — you just hand over a fresh description, and swiftui compares it to the old one and changes only what's different. the one thing that trips people up: your is throwaway. think of it as a recipe card, not the cake. swiftui re reads the recipe every time the data changes and bakes a fresh result. that's why views are cheap structs, and why you should never do slow work inside — the recipe gets read a lot . swiftui is a declarative ui framework. instead of writing step by step instructions to build your interface, you describe what the interface should look like for a given state. swiftui handles the how. declarative vs imperative imperative uikit — describe how you create an object. you set properties. you add it to a paren

t: 02 — Views and Modifiers: Building Blocks of SwiftUI
x: 02 — views and modifiers: building blocks of swiftui start here — the plain idea a modifier is just a method you tack onto a view with a dot — , — and here's the key surprise: it doesn't change the view, it wraps it in a new one. think of it like dressing someone: a is a coat, is a layer behind them. each thing you add goes around what came before. that wrapping is exactly why order matters. the lesson uses a gift wrapping analogy, and it's perfect: pad then background paints a bigger box; background then pad paints the small box and leaves clear space around it. same words, different order, different picture — because each modifier only ever sees the result of the one before it. once you notice you're applying the same little stack of modifiers over and over, you can bottle it up into a reusable and give it a friendly name like . it's basically a label that means "apply this exact outfit." and a handful of modifiers — like — quietly flow down to all children, the way a dress code applies to a whole room unless someone overrides it. every swiftui interface is built from views and modifiers. a view describes what appears on screen. a modifier transforms a view into a new view with different appearance or behavior. what is a modifier? a modifier is a method on that returns a new view wrapping the original. it does not mutate the existing view — it creates a new one. each modifier

lists and foreach start here — the plain idea a is the scrolling, row by row layout you see in settings, mail, and just about every ios app. is its partner: instead of typing out one row per item, you hand an array and a recipe for a single row, and it stamps out one row per element. think of as a cookie cutter — one shape, applied to every item in your data. for that stamping to work, swiftui needs to tell rows apart — which one got deleted, which moved. that's what and the parameter is for: it's a name tag on each item so swiftui can track them as the data shuffles. without a stable id, animations and updates get confused. the other thing worth knowing up front is that is lazy : it only builds the rows you can actually see, recycling them as you scroll. that's why a of 10,000 items stays smooth while a full of a giant would try to build all 10,000 at once. on top of that base, the lesson layers the conveniences — sections, swipe to delete, drag to reorder, pull to refresh, selection — but they're all decorations on the same list + foreach core. is swiftui's primary container for displaying scrollable, vertically arranged rows of data. it is backed by uitableview on ios and provides built in support for selection, swipe actions, editing, and lazy loading. is the companion construct that generates views from collections. see 04 collection types ../learning swift/04 collection t

navigation start here — the plain idea navigation is how a user drills from one screen into another and back — tap a list row, slide into its detail, tap the back arrow to return. swiftui models this as a literal stack of cards: is the deck, and pushing a screen drops a new card on top, popping takes it off. the back button is just "remove the top card." the modern, recommended way separates what you tapped from what shows up . a says "the user picked this item," and a elsewhere says "when someone picks an item of this type, show that screen." it's like a coat check: the link hands over a ticket the value , and the destination is the rule for what to bring back. this split is what makes programmatic navigation possible. that's where comes in — it's the stack of cards as a value you can edit in code. append to push, remove the last to go back, empty it to jump home. because the whole path is just data, you can save and restore it, which is how deep links and "reopen where i left off" work. navigation is how users move between screens in your app. swiftui's navigation system ios 16+ is built around and value based , replacing the older . the new system supports programmatic navigation, deep linking, and state restoration. see 09 enums ../learning swift/09 enums.md for enum based route definitions, which are the cleanest way to model navigation destinations. navigationstack is the

sheets and alerts start here — the plain idea where navigation slides you sideways into a new screen, modals pop up on top of what you're doing and ask for attention before letting you back. a sheet is the card that slides up from the bottom; an alert is the little "are you sure?" box; a confirmation dialog is the menu of choices that springs from the bottom. same family, different sizes. the clever part is that you don't "open" a modal directly — you flip a piece of state and swiftui presents it for you. means "whenever becomes , show this sheet; when it's , hide it." it's the same state drives the ui idea from earlier: the modal is just a reflection of a boolean or an optional item . to close it, you flip the state back, or call the action handed to you through the environment. there are two flavors worth noting. boolean presentation is for "show a sheet." item presentation is for "show the sheet for this specific thing " — set an optional to a value and the sheet appears carrying it; clearing it back to closes it. that item flavor is the clean way to, say, tap a row and open its editor. sheets, alerts, and popovers are swiftui's modal presentation tools. unlike navigation, which pushes screens onto a stack, modals appear on top of the current content and demand attention before the user returns. see 07 closures ../learning swift/07 closures.md for trailing closure syntax eve

drawing and shapes start here — the plain idea shapes are swiftui's drawing tools, and the happy surprise is that a shape is a view — a or drops into your layout exactly like a , and takes the same kinds of modifiers. you mostly do two things to a shape: it with a color paint the inside or it draw the outline . think fill = the crayon, stroke = the pen tracing the edge. when the built in shapes rectangle, circle, capsule, ellipse aren't enough, you draw your own with — and a path is just an invisible pen. you to lift and reposition the pen, to draw a straight segment, add arcs and curves, then to connect back to the start. a custom triangle or star is nothing more than a few pen moves described in code. the key habit for custom shapes is to draw relative to the you're given — the rectangle of available space — using , , and so on, rather than fixed numbers. that way your shape scales to whatever size it's placed in, like a stretchy stencil. once you have shapes, you also get clipping to mask a photo into a circle and gradient fills for free. swiftui provides a rich set of built in shapes and a api for drawing custom geometry. shapes conform to the protocol, which means they are views you can use them anywhere you would use or , and apply modifiers like , , and . see 13 protocols ../learning swift/13 protocols.md the protocol is a great example of protocol oriented design. you i

colors and materials start here — the plain idea in swiftui a color isn't just a paint swatch — it's a full fledged view and a "style" you can apply anywhere. the most important habit here is reaching for semantic colors like , , and the named set , . these aren't fixed hex codes; the system nudges their exact values so text stays readable in both light and dark mode. pick a color by its job , not its rgb, and dark mode mostly takes care of itself. gradients are colors that blend across space — straight line , outward from a center , or sweeping around like a clock . they all plug into the same spots a plain color does, because they share one umbrella idea, : anything that conforms can be used in , , or . so once you know where a color goes, you know where a gradient or material goes too. materials are the "frosted glass" effect — translucent layers that blur whatever's behind them, like the control center backdrop. they range from barely there to almost solid . think of a material as a privacy glass panel: it lets the background bleed through softly so your text on top still pops. color in swiftui is more than just decoration it is a view, a shape style, and a bridge to the system's dynamic appearance. this lesson covers system colors, custom colors, gradients, materials, and how everything adapts to dark mode. system colors swiftui provides named colors that adapt to the syst

animation basics start here — the plain idea here's the thing that makes swiftui animation feel like magic: you never describe the in between frames. you just change a value — from small to big, from here to there — and tell swiftui "make that change smooth ." it figures out every intermediate step for you by interpolating from the old value to the new one. animation is a label on a change that means "don't snap, glide." there are two ways to ask for that glide. implicit animation is attached to a view and says "whenever this value changes, animate it" — like a standing instruction. explicit animation wraps the moment you change the state and says "animate everything affected by this change, right now." reach for implicit when one view should always animate; reach for explicit when a single tap should ripple through several views at once. the personality of an animation comes from its curve . is robotic constant; the family starts and ends gently; and springs the recommended default overshoot a little and settle, which is why they feel alive and physical. two related ideas round it out: transitions describe how a view enters or leaves the screen fade, slide, scale , and makes one view appear to morph into another — the "hero" effect where a thumbnail grows into a full card. animation in swiftui works by interpolating between old and new values when state changes. you do not wri

animation advanced start here — the plain idea basic animation handled "go smoothly from a to b." this lesson is about animations with multiple beats — a bounce that compresses, stretches, overshoots, then settles. two tools cover that. a is like a flipbook of named poses: you list the phases, describe each pose, and swiftui auto animates from one to the next. a is the film editor's timeline: you lay down keyframes for each property offset, scale, rotation with exact timings, giving you frame level control. sf symbol effects are the easy win here — single modifiers that make apple's icons come alive: when something happens, for a heartbeat, to morph one symbol into another the heart filling in when you favorite something . pair them with and the phone gives a little haptic tap, so the animation is felt as well as seen. the most behind the scenes idea is the transaction . every animated change quietly carries a transaction — a note that travels down the view tree saying "use this animation." you can intercept that note to override it, or set it to to say "actually, don't animate this one." think of a transaction as the animation's shipping label; transaction control is you editing the label in transit. this lesson builds on animation basics with the powerful animation apis introduced in ios 17: phase animators for multi step sequences, keyframe animators for timeline based motio

gestures start here — the plain idea a gesture is just swiftui listening to your finger and telling you what it's doing. think of it as a tiny reporter standing on the screen: "they tapped here," "they're dragging — currently 40 points to the right," "they pinched to 1.5x." you attach the reporter to a view and react to its updates. the clever part is that gestures are plain values structs you can snap together like lego. want "long press, then drag"? you combine two gestures with an operator. want both pinch and rotate at once? another operator. behind the scenes swiftui is constantly deciding which gesture "wins" when several could match, and feeding you a object that describes the live state every frame. one thing worth holding onto early: is like a sticky note that automatically falls off when you lift your finger — it resets itself the moment the gesture ends. regular is a note that stays stuck until you remove it yourself. that single difference explains most "why did my drag snap back?" confusion. swiftui provides a rich gesture system that lets users interact with your app through taps, drags, pinches, and rotations. gestures in swiftui are structs that conform to the protocol, making them composable and type safe. see 10 structs vs classes ../learning swift/10 structs vs classes.md gestures are value types structs conforming to the gesture protocol. see 12 animation ba

observable and environment start here — the plain idea the core problem here is: how does a view know to redraw itself when your data changes? you don't want to manually refresh anything. so you mark a data class as "watchable," and swiftui quietly keeps an eye on it — when a property changes, any view reading that property redraws. is basically a label that means "spy on this class for me." the neat trick in the modern ios 17+ system is that it watches at the property level. think of it like a building where each room has its own motion sensor: a view only redraws if a property it actually reads changed. the old system was one big alarm for the whole building — touch any property and every observing view woke up, even ones that didn't care. that's why the new way is both simpler and faster. the is a second idea: a shared backpack that gets passed down the view tree. parents drop things in a theme color, a settings object , and any child reaching deep below can pull them out without every layer in between passing them along by hand. swiftui also fills that backpack with system stuff for free — dark mode, locale, a action. swiftui's data observation system has evolved significantly. the modern approach, introduced in ios 17, uses the macro to replace the older protocol. understanding both systems is essential since most codebases still contain legacy patterns. see 24 property wr

data flow start here — the plain idea data flow is really one rule wearing many costumes: state drives the screen, and changing state redraws the screen. you never reach in and "update the label" by hand. you change a value, and swiftui rebuilds whatever depended on it. this whole lesson is about deciding where that value should live and how other views get at it. the big principle is "single source of truth." picture every piece of data as having exactly one home address. other views either get a read only photocopy an init parameter or a live phone line to the original a — never their own private duplicate, because two copies drift apart and the ui goes out of sync. data flows down the view tree parent hands it to child , and actions flow up child calls a closure, parent decides what to do . think of it as water flowing downhill and messages shouted back uphill. when state gets too big or has real logic attached, you move it out of the view into an class — a "view model" — which is basically a labeled box that says "all the brains for this screen live here." and for values that must outlive the app session, is just a friendly wrapper that quietly reads and writes for you. swiftui's declarative paradigm relies on a strict data flow model: state drives the ui, and user actions modify state, which triggers re rendering. understanding where to place state, how to share it, and ho

grids and adaptive layout start here — the plain idea a grid is just a way of saying "lay these things out in rows and columns and figure out the spacing for me." instead of positioning every item by hand, you describe the recipe for a column once, and swiftui stamps your items into that shape automatically. the two families solve different problems. the lazy grids , are for big scrollable collections — and "lazy" literally means they only build the cells currently on screen, so a 10,000 photo grid stays smooth. the plain is the opposite: small, not scrollable, but it lines columns up perfectly across rows — think a spreadsheet or a form where labels and values must align. the real magic word is . tell it "each item is at least 100 points wide" and it does the math for you — fit 3 across on a phone, 6 across on an ipad, no statements needed. it's basically responsive web design built in. and when even that isn't enough, tools like try options until one fits and swap the whole layout at runtime let your screen reshape itself for whatever device it lands on. swiftui offers multiple grid systems: and for scrollable grids, for precise alignment based layouts, and layout protocols like and for adaptive design across device sizes. see 04 layout fundamentals ../learning swiftui/04 layout fundamentals.md stacks, frames, alignment, and spacing fundamentals. see 14 generics ../learning s

scrollview start here — the plain idea a is just a window onto content that's taller or wider than the screen. you put more stuff inside than fits, and it lets the user slide it around. that's the whole job. think of it as a long scroll of paper behind a small viewing slot. the catch that trips everyone up: a plain is eager — it builds every single child the instant the view appears, even the 9,000 rows nobody will ever scroll to. that's fine for a handful of items, disastrous for a big list. the fix is to wrap your content in a lazy stack / , which is like a stagehand who only sets up the scenery right before the audience can see it. build only what's on screen, recycle as you go. beyond that, most of this lesson is about steering the scroll: jumping to a specific item , knowing where you currently are , and making it snap nicely like a photo carousel . think of these as the difference between a free spinning wheel and one with gentle detents that click into place. is swiftui's container for scrollable content. unlike , it gives you full control over layout and appearance, but you must handle laziness and performance yourself by pairing it with or . see 07 lists and foreach ../learning swiftui/07 lists and foreach.md is a specialized scrolling container with built in cell styling, selection, and swipe actions. basic scrollview by default, scrolls vertically. its content is lai

tabview and toolbars start here — the plain idea think of an app's ui as a building. is the row of buttons at the bottom that takes you to entirely different floors — home, search, profile. toolbars are the little shelf of controls at the top or bottom of whatever floor you're currently on — share, edit, settings. one switches where you are ; the other gives you things to do here . is dead simple to start: hand it a few screens, give each a label icon + title , done. the moment you want to switch tabs from code — say a notification deep links to the search tab — you add a binding and each tab so swiftui knows which is which. using an enum for those tags is the clean way to keep it type safe. toolbars work by placement — you don't say "put this at x=300," you say "put this in the top trailing slot" or "the keyboard slot," and swiftui handles the exact position per device and platform. think of it as telling a host "seat them near the window" rather than pointing at a chair. one rule worth burning in: toolbars live inside a — no navigation container, no bar to hang them on. tab bars and toolbars are the primary navigation chrome in most apps. swiftui's manages top level screen switching, while the modifier places buttons, menus, and controls in navigation bars, bottom bars, and the keyboard area. see 08 navigation ../learning swiftui/08 navigation.md and for hierarchical navigati

search and filtering start here — the plain idea searching a list is really just two jobs glued together: a text box for the user to type in, and a rule that decides which items match what they typed. swiftui hands you the text box for free with — it draws the proper, native looking search bar in the navigation area, the same one you see in settings or contacts. you don't style it; you just say "this screen is searchable." the matching half is plain swift. you keep the search text in a variable, and you write a computed property that returns only the items whose names contain that text. the magic is that this property re runs automatically every time the text changes — swiftui watches your , and whenever it shifts, it re asks "what should the list show now?" and redraws. think of it as a spreadsheet cell: change one input, every formula that depends on it recalculates. everything else in this lesson suggestions, scopes, tokens, debouncing is just sugar on top of that loop. a scope is a second filter knob; a token is a tappable tag instead of typed text; debouncing is "wait a beat before doing the expensive work, so we don't search on every single keystroke." search is one of the most common interactions in any app. swiftui provides the modifier that adds a platform native search bar to navigation containers. combined with swift's collection filtering, you can build powerful, re

async patterns in swiftui start here — the plain idea some work is slow: fetching from the network, loading an image, reading a big file. if you did that work the normal, blocking way, the whole ui would freeze until it finished — taps ignored, scrolling stuck. async work is the fix: is just a label on a function that means "this might pause for a while," exactly like is a label that means "this might fail." when you something, you're saying "pause here politely and let the ui keep running; wake me when the answer arrives." the clever part in swiftui is tying that async work to the view's life. says "start this async job when the view appears, and — this is the gift — automatically cancel it if the view goes away." so if the user navigates back mid download, the request quietly stops; you never wrote cleanup code. think of as a job that's leashed to the view: view disappears, leash yanks, job stops. the rest of the lesson is patterns built on that idea: model the three honest states loading, loaded, error so the user never stares at a blank screen; for pull to refresh; for "load this picture and show a spinner meanwhile"; and as the rule "touch ui state only on the main thread." cancellation is the recurring theme — good async code checks "have i been told to stop?" before doing more expensive work. swiftui provides first class support for asynchronous work through modifiers li

geometry and preferences start here — the plain idea normally in swiftui you don't think about exact pixels — you describe what you want "center this," "fill the width" and the layout system figures out the sizes. it works like a conversation flowing two ways: the parent proposes a size downward, and each child reports back the size it actually wants. most of the time that's all you need. but occasionally you need the real numbers at runtime — how tall did this text end up? how far has this row scrolled? — and that's what this lesson is about. is your measuring tape. it hands you a that knows the size and position of its space. the catch — and it trips everyone up — is that a measuring tape that greedily grabs the whole room: takes all the space offered to it, unlike normal views that take only what they need. that's why the trick is to tuck it into an or with a clear color, so it measures quietly without shoving your layout around. preference keys solve the opposite direction problem. data normally flows down parent to child , but sometimes a child needs to whisper something back up — "hey, i turned out to be 40 points tall." a is the labeled channel for that upward message, and is the rule for combining messages when several children speak at once take the max, sum them, last one wins . the modern is the happy ending: geometry aware effects scaling, fading as you scroll witho

matched geometry & anchors start here — the plain idea normally each swiftui view lives in its own little box and doesn't know where the others are on screen. that's usually fine — but sometimes you want views to talk geometry to each other: a small thumbnail that grows into a full screen card, a highlight pill that slides between tabs, a line drawn between two buttons. that's what these two tools are for. is like telling swiftui "this little card and this big card are secretly the same object." you stamp both with the same name tag an inside a shared , and when one vanishes while the other appears, swiftui quietly animates the position and size from one to the other. you never compute a single coordinate — it does the in between frames for you. anchors solve the opposite but related problem: "i need to know where another view is, as a number, so i can draw or place something there." a child view can't normally report its frame to a far away parent. an is basically a sealed envelope — the child writes down its frame, passes the envelope up the tree, and a common ancestor opens it later and reads the exact rectangle in its own coordinate space. one is for morphing views, the other is for measuring them. two related tools for interactions that span more than one view : morphs a view from one place to another across a state change, and anchors let you read one view's frame and use

view composition start here — the plain idea think of building a ui like building with lego. you could try to mold one giant plastic shape for your whole house, but it'd be impossible to change and impossible to reuse. instead you snap together lots of small, simple bricks. view composition is exactly that: instead of one enormous , you write small focused views and plug them into each other. the problem it solves is keeping things readable and reusable as your app grows. a 300 line is a nightmare; ten little 20 line views, each doing one job, is a joy. and because each view is just a struct, passing one into another is as cheap as passing any value. behind the scenes, the magic glue is — a quiet compiler feature that lets you list several views in a row or use / and have them treated as one combined view. it's basically the thing that turns "here are three pieces" into "here's one block of ui." once you're comfortable with subviews, , and generics, you can build your own reusable containers that work just like apple's or . swiftui's power comes from composing small, focused views into complex interfaces. every view you write is a struct with a — and that body can contain other custom views. mastering composition means your code stays readable, reusable, and performant even as your app grows. see 14 generics ../learning swift/14 generics.md for generic type parameters used in r

uikit integration start here — the plain idea swiftui is the new way to build apple uis, but uikit is the older, bigger toolbox that's been around for over a decade. sometimes the thing you need — a map, a web view, the photo picker — only exists or works better in uikit. so apple gives you adapters that let the two worlds plug into each other, like a travel adapter that lets a foreign plug fit your wall socket. wrapping a uikit view into swiftui is ; wrapping a whole view controller is . both ask you for the same two things: "build it once" and "keep it in sync when my state changes" . think of it as a contract: swiftui says "i'll tell you when my data changes, you make the uikit object match." the tricky part is that uikit talks back through delegates callbacks like "the user just typed something" . swiftui has no idea what a delegate is, so you need a translator in the middle — that's the coordinator , a small class that catches uikit's callbacks and writes the result into your swiftui state. data flows down through and back up through the coordinator. and going the other direction — putting swiftui inside an old uikit app — you use , which is just a uikit view controller with a swiftui view living inside it. swiftui doesn't cover every uikit feature. when you need a uikit view like , , or a custom inside swiftui, you wrap it with . when you need a uikit view controller like

real world architecture start here — the plain idea knowing how to make a button or a list is like knowing how to lay a single brick. this chapter is about the blueprint — how all those bricks fit into a house that doesn't fall over when it gets big. the core problem it solves: as an app grows, code that "just works" in a small demo turns into a tangled mess where everything touches everything. the big idea behind almost every pattern here is the same: separate the things that change for different reasons. your ui how it looks , your logic what happens when you tap , and your data where it comes from want to live in different boxes. mvvm is just names for those boxes. dependency injection is just "hand a box its tools instead of letting it grab them off the shelf itself" — which makes it easy to swap a real network call for a fake one in tests. a router is "put all the navigation decisions in one place" so views don't have to know the whole map. think of it as going from a one room studio to a well organized house: every item has a drawer it belongs in, so when you need to find or fix something, you know exactly where to look. the rest of the chapter is just showing you which drawer is which. building a real app requires more than knowing individual swiftui views. you need patterns for organizing code, managing data flow, handling accessibility, optimizing performance, and stru

enzomanuelmangano/demos — animation reference for swift/swiftui/uikit start here — the plain idea this is a translation dictionary, not a tutorial. someone built 120+ gorgeous animations in react native, and it turns out the ideas behind them — the timing, the springiness, the way one scroll gesture drives ten visual properties — are the same no matter what tool you use. so this doc says "here's the cool effect, here's the trick that makes it work, and here's roughly how you'd write that trick in swiftui or uikit." the one mental model that unlocks most of these: a single number drives everything. you have one value — a scroll offset, a drag distance, a 0 to 1 "how expanded am i" progress — and you map that number onto scale, rotation, blur, opacity, position, all at once. that mapping is called in react native and shows up as or / in swiftui. once you see that, the imessage card stack and the expandable music player are the same idea wearing different clothes. the other half is springs. instead of animating "go from a to b over 0.4 seconds," you say "behave like a physical spring with this much bounce," and the motion feels alive. the translation tables at the bottom let you copy a feel snappy, bouncy, critically damped/no bounce straight across frameworks. think of it as a phrasebook: you don't need to speak react native, you just need to look up the phrase. 120+ react native

x: swiftui data flow — deep dive study plan start here — the plain idea you already know how to use , , and the environment. this series is about what's happening behind the glass. think of it as the difference between driving a car and knowing what the engine does when you press the pedal — same car, but now you can fix it when it sputters. the one idea everything hangs on: swiftui is a graph of who depends on what , not a screen you redraw. you change a value, and swiftui quietly figures out which views cared and refreshes only those. most data flow bugs are just a missing or unexpected connection in that graph — a wire you thought was plugged in that wasn't. this roadmap is the map. each lesson zooms into one piece of the machine, and lesson 10 snaps them back together into one trace you can follow end to end. how , , the observation framework, and the actually work together — how injection happens, and when and why it gets rejected. this is a mechanics first curriculum, not an api tour. the existing learning swiftui ../learning swiftui/00 roadmap.md lessons 05/15/16 teach you how to use these tools. this series teaches you how they work underneath so you can reason about any data flow bug in your codebase — especially the silent failures and the crashes. built because the codebase is data flow heavy. the goal is a precise mental model: who depends on what, how values travel do

01 — swiftui is a dependency graph start here — the plain idea think of swiftui like a spreadsheet. you don't tell a cell "recompute now" — you change one number, and every cell with a formula that mentions it updates by itself. swiftui works the same way: you change a value, and only the views that actually used that value redraw. here's the part that surprises people: the connection between a value and a view is created by reading it. if your view's reads , it's now subscribed to — and to nothing else. don't read it, and you're not subscribed, so changes can't reach you. almost every "my view isn't updating" bug is really "the wire i assumed was there was never created, because i never read the value." so under the hood there's no redrawing of trees. there's a graph of values and the bodies that depend on them, and a tiny bookkeeper that, on every change, asks: who read this? only those re run. the single most important mental model: swiftui is not a view tree that you redraw. it's a graph of values and the views that depend on them. when a value changes, swiftui re evaluates only the views that read it . everything in this series is a consequence of that one idea. the graph attributegraph underneath swiftui is a private framework called attributegraph . think of it as a spreadsheet: each attribute is a cell: a piece of state, a derived value, or a view's . edges are "depends

02 — view identity and lifetime start here — the plain idea the view struct gets thrown away and rebuilt constantly, so swiftui can't use "is this the same struct?" to decide whether your state carries over. instead it gives each spot in the ui a name tag — its identity . think of it as a hotel room number: the guests your view structs come and go, but the room number stays, and swiftui keeps your state in the room, not with the guest. same identity across an update → same room → your state is still there. new or changed identity → new room → fresh, empty state, old state thrown out. that's the whole story behind both classic bugs: state mysteriously resets the room number kept changing and state won't reset when you want it to same room, you just swapped the furniture . lifetime is just how long that room exists. while the identity is in the tree, its state lives; when the identity disappears, the state is destroyed. so is for stuff you're happy to lose when the view goes away. state persists because of identity . if you don't understand identity, you'll hit the two classic bugs: state that resets unexpectedly , and state that refuses to reset when you want it to . identity ≠ the view struct the view struct is recreated constantly lesson 01 . so swiftui can't use "is this the same struct instance?" to decide whether state carries over. instead it uses identity : a stable notio

03 — @state storage internals start here — the plain idea = swiftui keeps the real value in a box it owns; your view just holds a ticket to it. the box lives in the graph, keyed to the view's identity lesson 02 , so when the disposable view struct gets rebuilt, the new struct's ticket reconnects to the same box. that's why your counter doesn't reset to zero on every re render. the initializer you write is just the first time default. swiftui uses it once, when the box is first created, and ignores it forever after — so don't expect 's default to "reset" things later, and don't pour expensive work into it. one more thing happens behind the scenes when you write through : it both updates the box and tells the graph "anyone who read this is now dirty." that second step is the free ui update you never had to ask for. looks like a variable on a struct, but a struct is a disposable value lesson 01 . so where does the value actually live, and why does it survive? @state is a handle , not the storage is a property wrapper whose stored part is tiny: a reference to a box of storage that swiftui owns , allocated in the graph and keyed to the view's identity lesson 02 . the struct is recreated on every parent update. but 's storage box is not recreated — the new struct's handle is reconnected to the same box because identity is the same . that's why keeps its value across hundreds of re ru
