19 — metal performance shaders mps start here — the plain idea you've been hand writing shaders. mps is apple saying: "for the common stuff, don't bother — we already wrote it, and we tuned it for every chip we ship." think of it as a shelf of pre built power tools. need a gaussian blur, an edge detector, a histogram, an image resize? grab the tool, point it at a source texture and a destination texture, and let it run. what makes mps fast isn't magic — it's that apple's engineers rewrote each operation specifically for each gpu generation, picking the exact tricks that hardware likes. your custom shader is general purpose; theirs is hand tuned. so the rule of thumb is simple: if a built in does what you want, it'll beat your version. the pattern is always the same: you a filter onto a command buffer, which is just adding it to the gpu's to do list. and because they all share that list, you can chain them — blur, then sharpen, then scale — each feeding the next, all submitted in one go. apple's built in, hardware optimized gpu operations. use mps when a built in operation exists — it's always faster than your custom shader. image processing chaining operations in place processing some mps filters can process in place read and write same texture : when to use mps vs custom shaders use mps use custom standard blur gaussian, box variable blur, masked blur edge detection sobel cust

20 — variable blur start here — the plain idea a normal blur treats every pixel the same — smear everything by the same amount. variable blur is the small twist that makes it look professional: the blur amount changes across the image . sharp where it matters, soft everywhere else. that's the scroll edge fade at the top of a list, the dreamy out of focus background in portrait mode, the toy town tilt shift look. the trick is to give each pixel its own blur radius. where does that number come from? either a mask — a grayscale picture where white means "blur me a lot" and black means "leave me sharp" — or a formula based on position, like "the farther you are from the focus line, the blurrier you get." same blur loop as before; you've just made the radius a variable instead of a constant. behind the scenes the gpu still does the heavy thing — sample a circle of neighbors and average them — but now the size of that circle is decided per pixel. that's also why the performance tips matter: a big radius means a lot of neighbor reads, so you cap it, skip pixels the mask says are sharp, and for really heavy blur you cheat by blurring a shrunk down copy and scaling back up. different blur radius at different positions — the technique behind apple's scroll edge blur, depth of field, and tilt shift effects. concept standard blur: every pixel gets the same blur radius. variable blur: each

21 — glass and refraction start here — the plain idea glass in software is a magic trick — there's no actual glass, just clever lying about pixels. "frosted glass" is really a stack of small cheats layered together: blur the stuff behind it, pump up the color a bit so it doesn't look washed out, add a faint tint, drop in a soft highlight, and make it slightly see through. do all five and your brain says "that's glass." refraction is just bending the lookup: instead of reading the pixel straight behind the glass, you read a slightly shifted one, so the background looks warped. snell's law is only the recipe for how much to shift based on the angle and how dense the glass is. chromatic aberration is the same shift done three times — red, green, and blue each grabbed from a slightly different spot — because real glass splits colors at its edges. "liquid glass" is just all of these turned on at once over a rounded shape, with a moving highlight and darker, more reflective edges that edge effect is called fresnel — surfaces get shinier the more you look along them . the gpu runs the whole stack per pixel, every frame, which is why it feels alive. the techniques behind apple's frosted glass materials, liquid glass, and light refraction effects. frosted glass recipe apple's glass = backdrop blur + saturation boost + tint + specular highlight + compositing. refraction light bending chr

22 — lighting and normal maps start here — the plain idea a flat image is flat because nothing on it reacts to light. lighting fixes that, and the whole thing hinges on one idea: the normal — the direction a surface faces. lighting is just measuring how much that direction points at the light. face the light dead on, you're bright. turn away, you go dark. that single comparison a dot product is the heart of it. the phong model just adds three layers of this. ambient is the faint light that's everywhere so shadows aren't pitch black. diffuse is the "how much do i face the light" part — the matte shading. specular is the shiny hotspot you get when the light bounces off the surface straight into your eye. stack them and a flat circle suddenly looks like a lit ball. the clever part is the normal map . instead of giving every pixel the same facing direction, you store a different direction per pixel inside an image — packed as rgb, where red/green/blue encode the x/y/z of the direction. now a flat wall can have fake bumps and grooves, because each pixel catches the light as if it were tilted, even though the geometry never changed. you can even cook one up from a height map by looking at how steeply brightness changes from pixel to pixel. adding depth and dimension to flat surfaces using lighting calculations and normal maps. phong lighting model three components: ambient + diffuse

23 — animation in shaders start here — the plain idea a shader by itself is frozen — it computes the same picture forever. to make it move, you feed it one changing number: time . that's the whole secret. the gpu re runs your shader every frame, and if you make the output depend on , the picture changes frame to frame. animation = "draw, but with a clock ticking in one variable." the fun is in shaping that ticking number. raw time just climbs forever, so you fold it into useful patterns: to gently oscillate back and forth, to loop 0→1 over and over, to blink on and off. easing functions are just curves you bend that 0→1 progress through — ease in starts slow, bounce overshoots and settles — so motion feels physical instead of robotic. two more tricks round it out. stagger means offsetting time by position, so each row or item starts a beat later — that ripple down feel. and morphing blends two shapes by ing their math together as progress goes 0→1, so a circle smoothly melts into a square. all of it is just arithmetic on a single moving number. time based animation, easing, and morphing — making shaders come alive. passing time animation patterns oscillation easing in shaders staggered animation morphing between shapes looping and ping pong swiftui animation integration checkpoint x pass from swift, use and for animation x easing functions shape the animation curve x stagger =

24 — shader performance start here — the plain idea your shader doesn't run once — it runs for every pixel , millions of times per frame, 120 times a second. so a tiny inefficiency isn't tiny; it's multiplied into the millions. performance work here is just shaving cost off the thing that gets repeated the most. a few facts about the gpu explain almost every rule. it loves doing simple math in parallel, and it has special fast hardware for half precision numbers — so using instead of for colors is basically free speed. it hates branching: when threads hit an , the gpu often runs both sides and throws one away, so you replace with blending math like and . and reading from a texture is comparatively slow — each lookup costs — so the name of the game is fewer reads separable passes, sampling between pixels to get two for the price of one . the last danger is loops that don't know when to stop. a blur radius driven by user input could spin thousands of times and stall the whole gpu, so you cap it. everything else — profiling in xcode, downsampling before heavy effects — is just confirming where the cost actually is and cutting the biggest piece first. how to write shaders that run at 120fps on every device. profile, optimize, and avoid common pitfalls. the golden rules 1. use instead of — 2x faster on apple gpus for colors and visual effects 2. minimize texture reads — each read is

x: apple intelligence / siri glow — flowing edge light start here — the plain idea this is the colorful light that runs around the border of the screen when siri or apple intelligence is listening — the modern replacement for the old siri orb. like the other glow effects, it's drawn entirely with math, no images. the recipe is just three moves. first, a mask: each pixel measures how far it is from the nearest screen edge, and only pixels near the border get to glow bright at the edge, fading to nothing toward the center . second, color that travels : instead of a fixed hue, the color sweeps around the perimeter over time, with a couple of overlapping waves layered in so it shimmers and never looks mechanical. third, bloom: paint that color onto black and let overlapping light add up, so it reads as soft luminous glow. once you notice the pattern — distance mask + flowing color + additive bloom — you've basically got the whole family of apple glow effects. move the mask to the bottom edge and it's the ambient light from the previous lesson; wrap it around the border and it's this. when siri / apple intelligence is listening, a soft multi colour light flows around the edges of the screen . it's the spiritual successor to the old siri orb. the math is surprisingly approachable — a screen edge mask, a hue that flows around the perimeter over time, and additive bloom. apple's exact ver

t: 05 — Q3: How Does the User Touch It? (The *Interaction* Axis)
x: 05 — q3: how does the user touch it? the interaction axis two interactions can change the same thing q1 with the same driver q2 and still need different stacks — because of how the user touches them . this axis is the tiebreaker, and it's the one most often discovered too late. the three interaction models 1. fire and forget tap a button → it animates → done. the user cannot grab it mid flight. the animation runs to completion uninterrupted. any high rung works. swiftui , , . simplest case — no special machinery. examples: a checkmark drawing in, a toast sliding up, a screen push. 2. interruptible / redirectable the user can grab the thing while it's animating and send it somewhere else — and it must respond smoothly, carrying its current velocity, not snapping or restarting. this is the rung that plain declarative animation cannot do well. a in flight, if you start a new one, jumps. you need one of: — pause, reverse, retarget; designed for this. additive core animation — multiple animations compose instead of replacing, so a new one blends from the current presentation state. → apple reverse engineered/core animation smooth 60 120fps spring physics with velocity transfer wave — a new spring starts from the current value and current velocity , so redirection is seamless. → maths physics/damped harmonic oscillator examples: a sheet you can grab mid dismiss, a card mid fling, pip

t: Understand and Eliminate Hangs from Your App
x: understand and eliminate hangs from your app wwdc21 session 10258 presented by anubhav , os performance team at apple summary this session teaches how to identify, diagnose, and eliminate hangs periods of unresponsiveness where an app appears stuck, lags, or spins. it covers the fundamental architecture of the main thread and the run loop, explains why hangs happen, demonstrates diagnostic tools like time profiler and system trace, introduces metrickit for field diagnostics, and walks through common anti patterns and their fixes using a demo app called desserted a recipe app for drinks and desserts . what is a hang? a hang is when an app becomes unresponsive to user input. the user taps, swipes, or otherwise interacts, but nothing happens the ui freezes, animations stall, and the app feels broken. formal definition a hang is reported when the main thread is busy doing work or waiting on another thread or system resource , causing a delay in updating the view content by at least 250 milliseconds . why 250ms? research shows users perceive ui updates within 100ms as "instant" a delay of 250ms or more is definitively noticeable and frustrating events are buffered during a hang and cannot be handled, compounding the problem if the hang is long enough, the system watchdog will terminate the app entirely hang severity categories category duration user impact micro hang < 400ms brief s

wwdc18 session 803 designing fluid interfaces "when you align the interface to the way we think and move, something kind of magical happens. it really stops feeling like a computer, and starts feeling like a seamless extension of us." overview this session is widely regarded as one of the best wwdc talks ever delivered. presented by three members of apple's human interface team, it reveals the design philosophy and physics behind iphone x's gesture driven interface. unlike a typical design talk, it goes deep into the math and engineering behind making interfaces feel alive covering spring physics, rubber banding, momentum projection, and interruptible animations. the central thesis: an interface is fluid when it behaves according to the way people think, not the way machines think . fluid interfaces are responsive, interruptible, and redirectable. speakers speaker section focus chan karunamuni design principles core philosophy, the eight properties of fluid interfaces nathan de vries motion design spring physics, animation curves, momentum projection marcos alonso gesture design tap, swipe, one to one tracking, multi gesture handling part 1: interface design principles chan karunamuni chan lays out eight properties that make an interface feel fluid: 1. responsiveness minimized latency people are extremely sensitive to latency; even small delays break the illusion of direct mani

uikit internals — overview swiftui is the future, but it is a thin declarative skin over machinery that is still pure uikit and core animation underneath. every , every navigation push, every gesture you write in swiftui ultimately becomes a , a transition, or a . when an interaction misbehaves — a scroll that fights your gesture, a cell that recycles wrong, a hit test that misses — the answer is almost always one layer down, in uikit. this course is the substrate. it explains how uikit actually works so that the fluid interactions you build on top behave predictably. the mental model uikit is built from three intertwined systems: the view hierarchy decides what is drawn and where. the responder chain decides who handles events. the run loop decides when any of it happens. master those three and almost every uikit mystery dissolves. what you'll learn 1. 01 view controller lifecycle — the exact order of → → layout → , and why putting work in the wrong one causes bugs. 2. 02 responder chain — how touches and actions travel from a view up through controllers, and how and really resolve. 3. 03 uiscrollview mechanics — , , paging, deceleration, and the delegate callbacks that drive scroll linked effects. 4. 04 compositional layout — : items, groups, sections, and fractional sizing. 5. 05 diffable data sources — snapshot based updates that animate correctly and end an entire class of

view controller lifecycle a is a coordinator between a chunk of screen its and the rest of your app. its lifecycle methods fire in a precise order, and the single most common uikit bug is doing the right work in the wrong method. the order of events the two distinctions that matter most: runs once; runs every time the view appears. set up subviews and one time configuration in . refresh data that may have changed e.g. after returning from a pushed screen in . frames are not valid until . in , is often still the size from the nib/storyboard, not the real device size. never compute positions from in . why "don't read frames in viewdidload" matters can fire many times rotation, keyboard, trait changes , so keep it cheap and idempotent — assign frames, don't allocate. appearance is not loading a controller's can be loaded in memory without being visible . tells you the first; lets you touch the view without forcing it to load. accessing triggers immediately — a subtle cause of "why did my view load so early?" containment custom container controllers carousels, custom tab bars must keep the parent/child relationship correct or child lifecycle methods won't fire: skipping these calls is why a child's or rotation callbacks silently never run. related the view's geometry: layer geometry next: 02 responder chain · hub: 00 overview

the responder chain every interactive object in uikit — views, view controllers, the app itself — is a . events touches, presses, motion, remote control travel along a chain of responders until something handles them. understanding the chain explains hit testing, , with , and the keyboard. two phases: find, then bubble phase 1 — hit testing top down . when a touch lands, uikit walks the view hierarchy from the window downward to find the deepest view that contains the point and is eligible: a view is skipped if , , or . this is why a button "doesn't work" when an invisible overlay sits on top with interaction enabled, or why a subview drawn outside its parent's bounds can't be tapped doesn't matter — hit testing does . phase 2 — the responder chain bottom up . once the hit view is found, if it doesn't handle the event it passes it to its responder: the chain in practice actions ride this chain. this is the trick behind , , and : any responder that implements handles it. that's how the system "find" command or copy/paste reaches whatever is appropriate without hard wiring. first responder the first responder is the object that receives keyboard and text input. calling on a text field is what shows the keyboard; dismisses it. gates it. custom views can become first responder too — that's how you build a view that accepts key commands or shows an input accessory. why this matters

compositional layout ios 13+ replaced the pain of custom subclasses for 90% of cases. it is built from a strict, composable hierarchy — once you internalize it, almost any grid, list, carousel, or magazine layout becomes a few lines. the hierarchy: item → group → section an item is one cell. a group arranges items along one axis. a section stacks groups. a layout holds sections. fractional sizing is the superpower dimensions come in three flavors: / — relative to the container group or section . — fixed points. — a starting guess that self sizes to auto layout content great for text . because items size fractionally to their group , the same layout reflows correctly across screen sizes and orientations with no math on your part. per section layouts the real power: each section can have a completely different layout via the section provider, so a single collection view can mix a horizontal carousel, a grid, and a list: orthogonal scrolling carousels for free set the section's and the section scrolls horizontally inside a vertical collection view — the app store's shelves, with no nested scroll views: supplementary items headers, footers, and badges are pinned headers via and badges anchored to an item corner . try it adjust items per group, group count, spacing, and axis below to see how fractional sizing reflows the section. related feeding it data without crashes: 05 diffable

diffable data sources the classic / api made you responsible for keeping your model and the view in sync via . get the counts wrong and you crash: "invalid number of items." diffable data sources ios 13+ flip the model: you describe the desired state as a snapshot, and uikit computes the diff and animates it. snapshots describe state, not changes you never call / . you hand over the new truth; uikit figures out what moved, inserted, or deleted and animates each correctly. identifiers must be hashable and stable the diff is computed on identifiers , not objects. use a stable id not array index , and make sure equality is meaningful: store item identifiers in the snapshot, keep the full objects in your store, and look them up in the cell provider. this avoids re rendering everything when one field changes. if two different items hash equal, the diff misbehaves cells vanish or duplicate — the single most common diffable bug. cell registration replaces dequeue by string no more reuse identifier strings, no force casts. is the modern, value type way to fill a cell — the same configuration objects work for table and collection list cells. reloading vs reconfiguring ios 15+ updates a visible cell in place without replacing it — cheaper and flicker free for content changes where identity is unchanged. use it instead of when only the data inside a cell changed. why it matters diffable d
