core graphics & core image — overview below core animation's compositing of layers sits the layer that actually draws pixels : core graphics a.k.a. quartz 2d , a c based 2d drawing engine for paths, gradients, text, and images on the cpu. alongside it, core image is a gpu accelerated image processing pipeline of filters. together they cover everything between "arrange rectangles" core animation and "write a shader" metal . where each fits reach down into core graphics when a layer and image approach can't express the shape; reach into core image when you need to process an existing image rather than draw one. what you'll learn 1. 01 drawing model — the graphics context, the painter's model, and . 2. 02 paths and curves — / , lines, arcs, and bézier curves. 3. 03 gradients shadows blends — gradients, shadows, and blend modes. 4. 04 images and bitmaps — bitmap contexts, drawing & resizing images, scale and color spaces. 5. 05 core image filters — , the filter graph, and writing a . 6. 06 performance — when to draw, cache, or rasterize, and how to stay off the main thread. the mental model core graphics is a painter : you give a context a sequence of drawing commands and it lays paint down in order, each stroke covering what's beneath. core image is a darkroom : you describe a graph of filters and it processes an image through them on the gpu. how it connects the shapes you draw h

the drawing model core graphics draws into a graphics context — an opaque destination for drawing commands. the context can be the screen inside , an off screen bitmap, a pdf, or a print page. the same drawing code targets any of them. the painter's model core graphics is stateful and sequential . you set state fill color, line width, transform , then issue a paint command, and pixels are laid down immediately, on top of whatever is already there: order is everything: later commands paint over earlier ones. graphics state is a stack the context holds a graphics state colors, line attributes, the current transform, clipping . save it, change it, draw, and restore — like / : this is the same affine transform machinery as rotation matrices — is a 2d matrix. the modern way: uigraphicsimagerenderer you rarely call anymore. manages the context, scale, and color space correctly and returns a : it picks the right for the screen automatically, so your drawing is crisp on retina without manual / math. draw : vs rendering an image override when content changes with the view's bounds and you want uikit to manage invalidation . render to a once when the content is static and you'd rather hand a finished bitmap to an image view or a — that avoids re drawing on every layout. the trade off is covered in 06 performance. flipped coordinates uikit's gives you a context with the origin at the top

paths & curves a path is a sequence of lines and curves that you can stroke outline , fill interior , or use as a clip. is the low level type; is the friendly uikit wrapper. paths back , animated strokes, custom buttons, charts, and any non rectangular shape. building a path starts a new subpath without drawing. , , 1 control point , cubic, 2 control points . connects back to the subpath's start. the cubic bézier is the workhorse a cubic bézier blends four points — two endpoints and two "handles" that pull the curve toward them — exactly the formula from parametric curves: this same cubic is what , paths, font glyphs, and css/ timing curves are all made of. learn to feel how the handles steer the curve and you can draw or animate anything. fill rules when a path self intersects, the fill rule decides what's "inside": non zero winding default — counts the signed crossings; overlaps stay filled. even odd — toggles inside/outside on each crossing, creating holes. this is how you punch a hole a "spotlight" overlay : one big rect + an inner shape, even odd filled. stroking , / / , / / , and control the outline. dashes + animating from 0→1 produce the classic "draw on" signature/loading animation. path → cashapelayer animatable this bridges core graphics shapes into core animation; see cashapelayer. try it drag the four control points of the cubic bézier below and watch the tangent —

gradients, shadows & blend modes these three give flat shapes depth and richness. they're cheap to describe and, used well, are the difference between a "drawn rectangle" and a polished surface. gradients two kinds: axial linear, along a line and radial out from a center . in core graphics you draw a into a usually clipped region: for a view wide gradient that animates, prefer cagradientlayer — it's gpu composited. use core graphics gradients when the gradient must be clipped to a complex drawn path or baked into an image. shadows a shadow is offset + blur + color. set it on the context before drawing the shape it should apply to: for view layers, is the performance critical version — giving the shadow an explicit path avoids the expensive off screen pass uikit otherwise does to derive the shape. always set for moving/animating shadows. blend modes a blend mode decides how new paint combines with what's underneath — the same modes you know from photoshop and from shader compositing: is especially useful: draw an opaque shape with it and you cut that shape out of everything beneath — a one line way to build masks and "scratch off" effects. putting it together: a card surface a convincing card is often: a rounded rect path → clip → axial gradient fill → a subtle inner highlight stroke at the top → a soft on the layer. each piece is a few lines, and together they read as a real, l

images & bitmaps a is a high level, immutable picture; a is the raw bitmap underneath; a bitmap context is a canvas you can draw into and read back. knowing the difference is the key to resizing correctly, avoiding memory blow ups, and getting color right. scale: points vs pixels a carries a 1, 2, or 3 . its is in points ; the underlying pixel dimensions are . the number one image bug is ignoring scale — drawing at on a 3× screen and getting a blurry or mis sized result. handles scale for you: downsampling large images the memory trap loading a 12 megapixel photo into a 120pt thumbnail wastes 48 mb of ram per image — a top cause of memory pressure crashes in feeds. downsample at decode time with imageio so the full bitmap is never created: this is the single highest impact image optimization in list heavy apps. reading and writing pixels for per pixel work histograms, picking a color, generating textures , draw into a known format bitmap context and read the buffer: for heavy per pixel math, this is the slow cpu path — push it to core image or metal instead shaders . color spaces, briefly srgb is the safe default for ui. display p3 covers a wider gamut on modern displays richer reds/greens . mismatched color spaces cause subtle color shifts; when generating images for display, use unless you specifically want p3. related processing images with filters: 05 core image filters pre

core image filters core image ci is a non destructive, gpu accelerated image processing pipeline. you describe a graph of filters; ci fuses them into an optimized program and runs it on the gpu only when you ask for output. it's the bridge between core graphics drawing and full metal shaders. the three core types a is a promise , not a bitmap. chaining filters just builds a graph. nothing executes until a renders it — so a 10 filter chain costs one optimized gpu pass, not ten. chaining filters the big performance rule: create one and reuse it. allocating a context per render is the classic core image performance mistake. useful built ins blur , , color , , , stylize , , distortion , , generators , , and for arbitrary channel mixing. there are 200; lists them. real time rendering for live effects camera, scrubbing a slider , don't go through . render the straight into a metal backed view with a metal so the pixels never round trip through the cpu: this is how camera filters stay at 60fps. writing your own: cikernel when no built in fits, write a kernel in the metal shading language — a per pixel function, exactly like the fragment shaders in your fragment shaders notes: you load it as a / and apply it like any filter. this is the same per pixel thinking as your shader course, wrapped in ci's filter graph and memory management. try it the blur playground below is the canvas versi

drawing performance custom drawing is powerful and easy to make slow. the whole game is: draw as little as possible, as rarely as possible, off the main thread when you can, and let the gpu composite the rest. know what's expensive runs on the main thread and produces a bitmap the size of the view × screen scale. big views redrawn often = jank. off screen passes are the silent killer: + , less shadows, , and masks force the gpu to render into a separate buffer and composite it back. instruments' core animation tool flags these in yellow/red. the decision ladder most "i need custom drawing" turns out to be step 1 or 2. cheap shadows always set for any shadow that moves or animates — it can be the difference between 60 and 20 fps in a scrolling list. rasterize sparingly caches a layer as a bitmap — great for a complex static layer reused while scrolling, terrible for anything that changes it re rasterizes every frame . always pair with or it's blurry on retina. draw off the main thread works on a background queue. render expensive content there, then hop back to assign it: combined with the imageio downsampling from 04 images and bitmaps, this keeps scrolling smooth even with heavy thumbnails. measure, don't guess use instruments' core animation template color off screen rendered layers, color blended layers and time profiler to find the real cost. this pairs directly with instru

gestures & haptics — overview fluid interfaces feel alive because they respond continuously to the user and confirm actions physically . that's two systems working together: gesture recognition turning raw touches into intent and haptics turning events into felt feedback . this course covers both — the input side of the "feel" you keep chasing in your interaction work. the big idea a great gesture driven interaction is a pure function of the touch , not an animation that plays. the interface tracks the finger 1:1, and physics springs, deceleration takes over only on release. this is the thesis of apple's designing fluid interfaces, and it's why understanding gestures matters as much as understanding animation. what you'll learn 1. 01 gesture recognizers — the recognizer state machine, the built in recognizers, and discrete vs continuous. 2. 02 simultaneity and dependencies — making recognizers cooperate, fail, or require each other the hard part . 3. 03 velocity and rubber banding — velocity, projection, and the rubber band formula behind every apple scroll edge. 4. 04 core haptics — and the for custom textures. 5. 05 designing the feel — combining gesture, spring, and haptic into one coherent interaction. why it connects the math of velocity, projection, and rubber banding is interaction math applied. the spring that catches a released gesture is the same critically damped spr

gesture recognizers a watches the raw touch stream on a view and collapses it into a single, meaningful gesture — a tap, a pan, a pinch. it saves you from implementing by hand and, crucially, coordinates with other recognizers. the state machine every recognizer is a small state machine. the two shapes: discrete tap, long press : fires once or . continuous pan, pinch, rotation : or . you read , , , or depending on the recognizer — these are the continuous values you map to your effect. the built ins set , continuous after the press threshold , , , , discrete, directional , and interactive back swipe . translation reset trick accumulates from the gesture start. a common pattern is to read it, apply it, then zero it so you work in deltas: discrete vs continuous matters for design discrete gestures commit an action tap to select . continuous gestures track a value drag to move — and tracking is where fluidity lives, because the user stays in control the whole time. prefer continuous, interruptible gestures over discrete taps that trigger canned animations wherever the interaction allows. swiftui parallel swiftui's , , etc. wrap these same recognizers. when swiftui's gesture api isn't enough simultaneous recognition with a scroll view, precise failure requirements , drop to uikit via uiviewrepresentable. related the conflict resolution layer: 02 simultaneity and dependencies prev:

simultaneity & dependencies a single view often has several recognizers, and they compete for the same touches. by default, only one recognizer wins a touch sequence. coordinating them — letting some run together, making one wait for another to fail — is where most real gesture work happens, and it's all done through . the default: mutual exclusion if a view has both a tap and a pan, uikit lets exactly one recognize. a pinch and a rotation on the same view won't both fire unless you explicitly allow it. the fix is one delegate method: this is exactly how a photo viewer lets you pinch, rotate, and pan a picture at once. dependencies: require failure / require success two ordering tools: is the classic single vs double tap solution and the way you yield to system gestures at screen edges. cooperating with uiscrollview the trickiest real case: a custom pan inside a scroll view. the scroll view's pan and yours fight. options: implement to let both run, then decide in your handler. use to veto your recognizer based on direction e.g. only take over for horizontal drags, let vertical scroll pass through . shouldreceive: filtering touches lets you ignore touches that land on certain subviews e.g. don't start a background pan when the touch began on a button . it's the precise alternative to toggling everywhere. the hit testing connection before a recognizer ever sees a touch, hit testi

velocity & rubber banding the difference between a gesture that feels mechanical and one that feels alive comes down to two things: respecting the velocity of the finger when it lifts, and resisting — with rubber banding — when the content reaches a boundary. both are simple formulas, and both are everywhere in ios. velocity: the handoff to physics when a drag ends, the finger has a velocity points/second . a canned animation ignores it; a fluid one continues the motion at that speed and lets a spring or deceleration take over. this is the gesture→physics handoff. projection: where would it land? uiscrollview's deceleration uses an exponential decay. the closed form "final resting point" is the standard projection function — use it to decide which page/item the flick should snap to: this is the math from designing fluid interfaces — project the flick, find the nearest snap target, then spring there. rubber banding when content is dragged past its bounds top of a scroll view, edge of a card stack , it shouldn't move 1:1 — it should resist , moving less and less the further you pull. apple's formula: the result asymptotically approaches a limit no matter how hard you pull — exactly the bouncy resistance of every ios scroll edge. on release, a spring returns it to the boundary. the complete release behavior that three case release is the heart of nearly every premium ios interacti

core haptics haptics are the felt half of feedback — the tap you feel when a picker lands, the buzz when an action fails. used well they make an interface feel physical and confident; overused they're annoying. ios gives you two tiers: simple semantic generators and the full core haptics engine. tier 1: uifeedbackgenerator use this 90% of the time three semantic generators cover most needs. they map to system tuned patterns and respect the user's settings: the key detail: call just before you expect to fire. it wakes the taptic engine so the haptic lands in sync with the visual; without it there's a perceptible lag. match haptics to motion a haptic that fires a frame off from its visual feels wrong. trigger the feedback at the exact moment the visual event happens — the snap, the toggle, the boundary hit from 03 velocity and rubber banding. a pull to refresh, for example, fires one precisely when the threshold is crossed, not when the release animation finishes. tier 2: chhapticengine custom textures for bespoke feedback — a continuous rumble that tracks a drag, a layered "texture" — use core haptics directly. you build a pattern of transient events sharp taps and continuous events sustained vibration , each with and : intensity — how strong amplitude . sharpness — timbre, from soft/round 0 to crisp/clicky 1 . you can drive a continuous event's parameters in real time a so the

designing the feel this is the synthesis lesson: take a gesture, a spring, and a haptic and combine them into one interaction that feels like a physical object. everything in this course and your interaction math notes comes together here. the recipe track the finger 1:1 while it's down. resist at boundaries with rubber banding. on release, continue with the finger's velocity into a spring. confirm discrete moments with a haptic. that's it — four ingredients, applied consistently, are what separates "apple grade" from "fine." a worked example: a dismissable sheet notice: the decision uses both displacement and velocity a fast flick dismisses even a short drag , the haptic fires at the moment of commitment , and both outcomes are springs seeded with the release velocity. principles that scale interruptible always. the user can grab the sheet mid dismiss and drag it back. springs are retargetable; canned animations aren't — this is why you prefer springs springs . velocity is sacred. never drop the finger's velocity at release; it's the single biggest contributor to "fluid." haptics mark events, not motion. one crisp tap at the threshold beats a continuous buzz. tune by feel, then derive. find the spring response/damping that feels right, then note the numbers — the workflow from the study method. what to avoid fixed duration animations for gesture driven ui they fight interrupti

widgetkit & live activities — overview widgets, live activities, and the dynamic island are some of the highest craft surfaces on ios — tiny, glanceable, and unforgiving of sloppiness. they share one runtime model: your code runs briefly, produces swiftui views and a schedule, and the system renders them later, without your app running. the fundamental constraint a widget is not a live app. you don't get a render loop. you hand the system a timeline of pre rendered states with dates, and it shows each one at the right time. no timers, no networking at display time — everything is computed in advance. internalizing this "archived snapshots, not a running app" model explains every widgetkit rule. what you'll learn 1. 01 timeline provider — the , snapshots, entries, and refresh policies. 2. 02 widget views families — building the swiftui view, supported families, and the rules. 3. 03 live activities — activitykit: live, updating content on the lock screen. 4. 04 dynamic island — the compact, minimal, and expanded presentations. 5. 05 interactivity and app intents — buttons and toggles in widgets via app intents, and deep links. where it runs all of this lives in a widget extension — a separate target/process from your app, with its own memory budget. sharing data with the main app means an app group container shared or a shared swiftdata/file store , covered alongside persistence.

the timeline provider the is the engine of a widget. it answers one question: "what should this widget show, and when should it change?" you return a list of timed entries; the system caches them and renders each at its date — no further code of yours runs in between. three methods each is a with a when it becomes visible plus your data. refresh policies the tells the system when to ask for a fresh timeline: — request a new timeline after the last entry's date. — refresh at a specific time. — only when the app explicitly reloads it. you don't control timing precisely the system budgets widget refreshes battery, frequency caps . a timeline asking for updates every minute will be throttled. design around it: pre compute several entries so the widget appears to update frequently from a single refresh, and push urgent changes from the app with: getting data in because the provider runs in the extension, it reads from the shared app group store the main app writes to — not from your app's memory. fetch/network in the app, persist to the shared container, and let the provider read it synchronously. heavy work in risks the extension being killed for exceeding its memory budget. configurable widgets user customizable widgets choose a city, pick an account use with an configuration — the same app intents system from 05 interactivity and app intents and app intents & siri. related what t

widget views & families a widget's appearance is a swiftui view fed one timeline entry. the discipline is making one view adapt across several families sizes , and following the platform rules that keep widgets crisp and legible. the widget definition adapt to the family the same view renders at very different sizes. read the environment and branch: families are the lock screen / watch complications — monochrome, vibrancy tinted; design them as glyphs and gauges, not full layouts. the rules that trip people up is required ios 17+ — it's how the system insets and renders your background in standby, the gallery, and on the home screen. without it the widget looks broken. no scrolling, no video, no interactive uikit. widgets are static images plus since ios 17 / backed by app intents — nothing else is interactive. use system fonts and so tinted home screen modes recolor your widget correctly. keep the view pure — it's rendered from an archived entry; it can't fetch or run timers. animation widgets get limited , system driven transitions between timeline entries ios 17+ : swiftui will animate numeric text and view changes between entries automatically, and you can hint with . you don't drive these frame by frame — you describe two states and the system tweens, the same declarative model as swiftui animation. related the data that feeds it: 01 timeline provider live, updating varian

live activities a live activity is a glanceable, updating view on the lock screen and in the dynamic island — a ride approaching, a game score, a timer, a delivery. it's widgetkit's views plus a live update channel activitykit , bounded by strict time and size limits. the shape you define the activity's data in two parts: attributes fixed for the activity's life and content state the part that updates : lifecycle: start, update, end updating without your app running the power move is remote updates via push : each activity has a ; your server sends a special push with the new , and the system re renders the views — your app never wakes. this is how a delivery or sports app keeps the lock screen current. local updates work too but only while your app has execution time. the views the live activity declares a swiftui layout for the lock screen banner and for the dynamic island presentations next lesson . they're the same view building skills as 02 widget views families, under even tighter size limits. constraints to design around live activities have a maximum lifetime hours, then the system ends them , a small memory budget, and update throttling. keep tiny, set sensible s so the ui can show "outdated" gracefully, and always provide an path. related the dynamic island presentations: 04 dynamic island prev: 02 widget views families · hub: 00 overview

the dynamic island the dynamic island is the live, morphing pill around the front camera. it's the presentation layer of a live activity, and it's a masterclass in fluid motion — shapes that grow, merge, and split with physical springs. you author three discrete states; the system handles the morphing between them. the three presentations compact — the default: small leading + trailing content hugging the camera. minimal — a single tiny glyph, used when multiple activities compete for the island. expanded — a rich layout shown on long press or for momentary alerts. you describe states; the system animates you never animate the pill morphing. you declare what each state looks like and the system tweens between them with its signature springs — the same "two states, let the system interpolate" model as swiftui animation animation basics and the wwdc spring talk animate with springs . your job is making each static state beautiful and legible. design constraints the hardware camera cutout is sacred — content flows around it; never assume a rectangle. compact content is tiny: a glyph and a few characters. reserve detail for expanded. use , vibrancy, and sf symbols; the island is rendered with system materials. keep the leading/trailing compact items visually balanced — they read as one unit split by the camera. tap targets and deep links tapping the island/live activity launches yo

interactivity & app intents before ios 17 a widget could only deep link into the app. now a widget or live activity can contain s and s that run code in place — check off a reminder, toggle a light — without launching the app. the mechanism is app intents . buttons backed by intents a widget button doesn't take a closure there's no running app to run it . it takes an — a serializable description of an action the system performs on your behalf: when tapped, the system runs in your extension, you mutate the shared store , and widgetkit reloads the timeline to reflect the change — all without opening the app. why app intents, not closures the widget ui is an archived snapshot; there's no live code to call. an is a value the system can serialize, schedule, and execute in a fresh process. that same property is why app intents also power siri, shortcuts, and spotlight — one definition, many entry points covered in app intents & siri . deep links for everything else for actions that genuinely need the app open a detail screen , use a url: handle it in the app with . keep perform fast and shared state based runs with a tight budget. do the minimal mutation against the app group store; don't network or do heavy work there. if a button needs network, mutate optimistic state, reload the timeline, and let the app reconcile later. related the intents system in full: app intents & siri share

networking — overview almost every app talks to a server. apple's is a capable, modern http stack — with async/await it's genuinely pleasant, and you rarely need a third party library like alamofire for typical rest work. this course builds a clean, testable networking layer from the primitives up. what you'll learn 1. 01 urlsession — the session, requests, configurations, and the response model. 2. 02 async await requests — modern request code, cancellation, and a typed request layer. 3. 03 codable — modelling json with , keys, dates, and nested/odd shapes. 4. 04 errors and retries — a real error taxonomy, status code handling, retries and backoff. 5. 05 caching auth and testing — , auth tokens, and testing the layer with . the mental model a request is a value , a session is the engine , and a response is data + metadata . your job is to turn a typed request into a typed model, handling failure honestly at every step. the shape of a good networking layer keep the transport sending bytes separate from modelling codable and policy retries, auth . that separation is what makes the layer testable. how it connects networking is almost entirely async, so it leans on the async/await model and structured cancellation. the decoded models often flow into swiftdata for offline use, and the whole layer is a prime target for the dependency injection in app architecture so you can swap in

urlsession is the engine. it manages a pool of connections, applies a configuration caching, timeouts, headers , and hands you data via tasks. understanding its pieces means you almost never reach for a third party http library. sessions and configurations three configuration flavors: disk cached, credential storage , nothing persisted — "private" sessions , and uploads/downloads that continue after the app is suspended, delivered to your app on relaunch . building a request a is a value you configure: build urls safely with so query items are percent encoded correctly: the response is data + metadata a request returns . the is really an carrying the status code and headers — and a non 2xx status is not an error as far as urlsession is concerned. a 404 or 500 still "succeeds" at the transport level; you must check the code yourself covered in 04 errors and retries : task types in memory , the common case , large bodies/files, progress , and streams to a file on disk — use for large payloads to avoid loading them into ram . with async/await you mostly use , , and . related the modern call style: 02 async await requests prev: 00 overview · hub: 00 overview

async/await requests 's async methods turn callback spaghetti into linear, readable code that supports cancellation for free. this lesson builds a small, typed request layer you'd actually ship. the basic call no completion handlers, no nesting, errors propagate with . call it from a or another async function. a typed endpoint layer don't scatter url strings across the app. describe endpoints as values and have one function execute them — this is the seam you'll later mock for tests and inject per dependency injection: cancellation is structured an async request is tied to its . cancel the task or let it cancel when its scope ends and the underlying request is cancelled — no manual bookkeeping: this is why search as you type is trivial with async/await: cancel the in flight task on each keystroke. it builds directly on structured concurrency. parallel requests need several resources at once? runs them concurrently and awaits together — total time is the slowest request, not the sum: related the decode step: 03 codable concurrency underneath: structured concurrency prev: 01 urlsession · hub: 00 overview

codable = is swift's built in serialization. for json apis it turns bytes into typed models with almost no boilerplate — and gives you precise escape hatches when the json is messy. the happy path if property names match the json keys and types line up, that's the whole story. key mismatches: keydecodingstrategy apis love . rather than annotate every property, convert globally: for one off renames, : dates and other strategies centralize these in one configured decoder and reuse it everywhere so every model decodes consistently. messy json: custom init from: when the shape doesn't map cleanly — a field that's sometimes a string and sometimes a number, a flattened nested object, defaults for missing keys — implement the decoder yourself: decoupling api shape from your domain a useful pattern: decode a that mirrors the json exactly, then map it to a clean domain model. your app code never depends on the api's quirks, and when the api changes you fix one mapping function. this dto→domain boundary pairs with clean data flow. related where it slots in: 02 async await requests persisting decoded models: swiftdata prev: 02 async await requests · hub: 00 overview

errors & retries networking fails constantly — flaky connections, expired tokens, server hiccups, rate limits. production grade code treats failure as a first class path, with a clear error taxonomy and a retry policy that doesn't make things worse. a real error taxonomy collapse the many failure modes into a domain error your ui can reason about: map raw failures into it: status codes carry meaning retry only what's retryable — with backoff retrying a 400 your bad request is pointless; retrying a 503 or a timeout is sensible. use exponential backoff with jitter so a recovering server isn't hammered by every client at once: for , honor the server's instead of your own backoff. surface failure honestly in the ui, distinguish offline show a retry affordance , cancelled show nothing — expected , decoding a bug — log it, don't silently swallow , and server transient — retry then apologize . mapping each case to a specific ui state is what makes an app feel robust rather than mysteriously broken. related token refresh on 401: 05 caching auth and testing sleeping/awaiting: async/await prev: 03 codable · hub: 00 overview

caching, auth & testing three pieces that turn a working request into a production layer: caching speed + offline , authentication tokens done right , and testing proving it works without hitting a real server . caching with urlcache has an http cache out of the box. if the server sends proper / headers, responses are cached and revalidated automatically: for app level caching offline browsing, instant reloads , persist decoded models into swiftdata and treat the network as a refresh — the "read from store, revalidate in background" pattern. authentication tokens the common scheme: a short lived access token sent on every request, and a long lived refresh token used to get a new access token when it expires 401 . the hard part is doing the refresh once even when many requests fail at the same moment: using an here is exactly the data isolation tool from actors — it serializes access so two requests can't kick off two refreshes. never store tokens in ; use the keychain shipping covers secure storage alongside it . testing without a server: urlprotocol the cleanest way to test networking is to intercept requests with a custom and return canned responses — fast, deterministic, no network: alternatively, hide the network behind the protocol from 02 async await requests and inject a fake implementation — usually simpler for unit tests. both approaches are the testing seam covered in

persistence & swiftdata — overview every app needs to remember things — between launches, offline, across devices. ios gives you a ladder of options from a single value to a full object graph. the skill is matching the tool to the data, and knowing the core data engine that still sits under the modern api. the ladder of options don't reach for a database to store a boolean, and don't store a thousand related records in a json file. pick the lightest tool that fits the shape and size of the data. what you'll learn 1. 01 where data lives — userdefaults, the sandbox directories, and what belongs where. 2. 02 swiftdata — the modern api: models, queries, the context. 3. 03 core data engine — what swiftdata is built on, and when you still meet core data. 4. 04 files and codable — saving codable to disk and sharing via app groups. 5. 05 migrations and performance — evolving your schema safely and keeping it fast. the mental model persistence has two halves: a store where bytes live and a context your in memory working set that you mutate and then save . swiftdata and core data both follow this; understanding it explains saving, undo, and threading. how it connects decoded network models codable often land in swiftdata for offline use. widgets read a shared store via an app group widget interactivity . and the models swiftdata produces plug straight into the observation framework.

where data lives before reaching for a database, know the lightweight stores and the app sandbox. putting data in the wrong directory is a real bug — it can get purged, fail to back up, or get your app rejected. userdefaults / @appstorage for small, simple values — preferences, flags, the last selected tab. backed by a property list, loaded into memory at launch. is a property wrapper that reads/writes and invalidates the view when it changes — the data flow integration from state storage. rule: small values only. it's not for images, large arrays, or models. the app sandbox directories your app can only read/write inside its sandbox. the directories differ in backup and purge behavior — this is the part people get wrong: common mistakes: putting a cache in bloats the user's icloud backup → possible rejection , or putting the database in the os deletes it and the app "loses everything" . excluding from backup for large re downloadable files you keep in application support, mark them so they don't inflate backups: keychain for secrets tokens, passwords, keys — never it's plain plist . the keychain is encrypted and survives reinstalls optionally . the api is c ish; most apps wrap it or use a tiny helper: app group: sharing with extensions widgets, share extensions, and your app are separate processes. to share data, put it in an app group container enabled in capabilities — a dir

swiftdata swiftdata ios 17+ is apple's modern persistence framework: you annotate plain swift classes with , and they become a queryable, relational, observable store — with deep swiftui integration. it's the recommended default for structured data in new apps. define a model rewrites the class to track changes and persist properties. relationships are just typed properties; means deleting a task deletes its subtasks. the container and context in swiftui you wire the container once and get a context from the environment: @query is the magic runs a fetch and keeps the view in sync: insert, delete, or edit a model anywhere and every showing it updates automatically. it's the persistence equivalent of the dependency graph — you describe what you want, the framework keeps it current. filter and sort declaratively: saving, deleting, undo and stage changes; swiftdata autosaves by default, or call explicitly. enable undo with for free edit/undo. threading the main context is main actor bound. for heavy imports, use a background via , do the work off main, and save — the same main actor discipline from @mainactor discipline. pass model ids across actors, not the model objects. related the engine underneath: 03 core data engine prev: 01 where data lives · next: 03 core data engine · hub: 00 overview 🆕 staying current ios 26 / wwdc26 swiftdata kept growing after its ios 17 debut — these

the core data engine swiftdata is a modern swift skin over core data , the framework that has powered ios persistence for over a decade. you'll still meet core data in existing codebases, in advanced scenarios swiftdata doesn't yet cover, and in the concepts that explain swiftdata's behavior. the core data stack swiftdata's / map directly onto / . same architecture, friendlier api. managed objects are faulted core data is lazy. a fetched object can be a fault — a placeholder that hasn't loaded its data yet; touching a property fires the fault and loads from sqlite. this makes fetching large graphs cheap but means i/o can happen at surprising times. swiftdata inherits this laziness, which is why you keep model access on the right actor. contexts and threading the classic footgun a managed object belongs to the context that created it and must not be used on another thread. cross threads by passing the swiftdata: and re fetching in the target context. the standard pattern is a main queue context for ui and background contexts for imports, merging changes between them. swiftdata automates much of this, but the rule is the same and explains its actor constraints from 02 swiftdata. when you still need core data directly fine grained fetch control: efficient table/collection backing , batch updates/deletes that skip loading objects into memory. complex migrations beyond swiftdata's c

files & codable storage sometimes a database is overkill. for documents, exported data, caches, and sharing between your app and its extensions, writing values straight to disk is simpler, transparent, and fast. codable → disk in three lines writes to a temp file and renames — so a crash mid write never corrupts the existing file. for large or performance sensitive data, prefer binary plist or a compact binary format over json. choosing the directory use the right sandbox location from 01 where data lives: user documents in , app managed data in , throwaway/recreatable data in . create the directory before writing: sharing via app groups to hand data to a widget or share extension, write into the app group container both processes can reach: this is the concrete mechanism behind the timeline provider reading your app's data. large binaries: write files, store paths don't stuff images or video into json/swiftdata as — it bloats the store and memory. write the binary to a file and persist only its filename/relative path in your model. swiftdata's automates exactly this: large properties are written to separate files and referenced, keeping the database lean. document based apps if the file is the document a drawing app, an editor , use swiftui or uikit . they handle reading/writing, conflict resolution, autosave, and icloud drive integration — don't hand roll that for true docume

migrations & performance two things separate a toy store from a shippable one: it can evolve without losing user data when you change the schema, and it stays fast as data grows. both need a little upfront thought. why migrations matter the moment you ship, users have data on disk in your current schema. add a property, rename an entity, or change a relationship, and the new app's schema no longer matches the old store. without a migration plan, the store fails to open — and the app crashes on launch or wipes data. swiftdata versioned schemas swiftdata expresses each schema version as a type, and a migration plan describes how to move between them: lightweight migrations are automatic: adding/removing optional properties, adding entities. no code needed beyond declaring the stage. custom migrations run a closure to transform data split a name field, compute a new value when the change isn't mechanical. the same lightweight vs heavyweight distinction exists in core data; swiftdata just makes the common case declarative. always test a migration by installing the old version, adding data, then upgrading. performance: fetch less, fetch lazily predicate at the store, not in swift. filter with so sqlite does the work and returns few rows — never fetch everything and in memory. sort in the query , paginate with / for large lists. only load what you show. core data faulting means relat

concurrency in practice — overview swift concurrency async/await, tasks, actors replaced the old world of callbacks and locks with a model the compiler can check for data races. your swift concurrency basics notes cover the syntax; this course is about using it correctly in a real app — the patterns, the pitfalls, and the discipline that keeps the main thread smooth. the three pillars layered on top: for ui thread safety, and — the compiler's check that a value is safe to cross concurrency boundaries. the mental shift old model: you pick a queue and hope you don't touch shared state from two threads. new model: you mark what must run where , actors , and the compiler refuses to let you create a data race. this is the same move as elsewhere in modern apple dev — declare intent, let the system enforce correctness. what you'll learn 1. 01 async await model — what / actually do suspension, not blocking and tasks. 2. 02 structured concurrency — , task groups, and cancellation propagation. 3. 03 actors and isolation — actors, reentrancy, and . 4. 04 mainactor discipline — keeping ui on the main actor without freezing it. 5. 05 pitfalls and patterns — the mistakes everyone makes and the patterns that avoid them. how it connects this underpins networking every request is async , swiftdata context actor isolation , and swiftui itself , views . strict concurrency checking — on by default

the async/await model / lets you write asynchronous code that reads top to bottom, like synchronous code, while never blocking a thread. the key is understanding what actually does — it's a suspension point , not a pause. suspension, not blocking when you , the function may suspend : it gives up its thread, lets other work run, and resumes later — possibly on a different thread — when the awaited result is ready. no thread sits idle waiting. contrast with old blocking code that ties up a thread, or callback code that fragments logic across closures. here the control flow is linear and errors use ordinary / . tasks: the unit of async work functions can only be called from an async context. a is the bridge from sync like a button handler into async: a runs concurrently with the code that created it. it captures the current actor context a inside a view runs its body on the main actor unless you say otherwise . await ≠ parallel two sequential s run one after another . to run work concurrently you must say so with or a task group 02 structured concurrency . a common beginner mistake is expecting two awaits to overlap — they don't. where async runs by default, functions don't promise a thread — they run on the cooperative thread pool, sized to your cpu. ui touching code must hop to the main actor 04 mainactor discipline . cpu heavy work should not run on the main actor; let it run o

structured concurrency structured concurrency means child tasks live within a scope : they can't outlive it, errors propagate out of it, and cancelling the parent cancels the children. this structure is what makes concurrent swift code safe and leak free, compared to fire and forget threads. async let: a few concurrent children starts work immediately and binds a promise; you it where you need the value. all s in a scope run concurrently: total time is the slowest request, not the sum. if the scope exits or throws before awaiting, the unawaited children are automatically cancelled — no leaks. task groups: a dynamic number of children when the count is dynamic one task per item , use a task group: the group won't return until every child finishes or one throws and the rest are cancelled . to bound concurrency don't launch 1,000 at once , add tasks up to a limit, then add a new one each time one completes. cancellation is cooperative cancellation doesn't forcibly stop a task — it sets a flag. well behaved code checks it and stops early: and already check cancellation, so awaiting them propagates cancellation automatically. this is why cancelling a search on each keystroke networking just works — and why a long cpu loop should call periodically or it ignores cancellation. why "structured" matters because children are bound to a scope, you can reason locally: when returns, nothing

actors & data isolation an actor is a reference type that protects its mutable state by allowing only one task inside at a time. it's swift's structural answer to data races: instead of remembering to take a lock, the type system guarantees serialized access. the problem actors solve two tasks mutating the same from different threads is a data race — undefined behavior, crashes, corruption. the old fix was manual locks , serial queues , easy to get wrong. an actor makes the compiler enforce it: can only be touched through the actor, and only one task runs the actor's code at a time. no locks, no races. crossing in is async from outside , every access is — because you might have to wait your turn: inside the actor, its own methods call each other synchronously you're already isolated . reentrancy: the subtle part actors are reentrant — when an actor method s, it suspends and the actor can run other tasks in the meantime. so state you read before an may have changed after it: guard against duplicate work by storing the in flight task a , so concurrent callers await the same download — the coalescing pattern from token refresh. "check your assumptions after every await" is the actor rule. sendable: what's safe to cross boundaries a value sent into or out of an actor/task must be — provably safe to share. value types of parts are automatically ; es with immutable state can be marke

@mainactor discipline all uikit and swiftui updates must happen on the main thread. is how swift concurrency expresses and enforces that. the discipline is: keep ui on the main actor, push heavy work off it, and let results flow back — without ever freezing the main thread. what @mainactor means annotates a type, method, or property as running on the main thread. the compiler then checks it — calling main actor code from a background context requires an hop, and accidentally touching ui off main becomes a compile error rather than a runtime glitch. swiftui s are implicitly , as are most of uikit. so a started inside a view runs its body on the main actor by default. the trap: heavy work on the main actor because in a view inherits the main actor, cpu heavy work inside it blocks the ui : move the heavy part off main, then return to main only to publish: or put the heavy function on a background actor / make it . the principle: the main actor is for touching ui, not for computing. nonisolated for pure work if a method on a type does no ui work, mark it so it can run anywhere without forcing a hop: awaiting back to main you rarely call anymore. returning from an into main actor code resumes on the main actor automatically. when you do need an explicit hop from a non isolated context: the payoff done right, means "ui off the main thread" bugs become compile errors, and "the app fro

pitfalls & patterns a field guide to the concurrency mistakes everyone makes once, and the patterns that prevent them. pitfall: expecting sequential awaits to be parallel fix: or a task group when the work is independent 02 structured concurrency . pitfall: forgetting cancellation a long task keeps running after the user leaves the screen, wasting battery and possibly updating dead ui. fix: store the and cancel it, or use swiftui's modifier, which cancels automatically when the view disappears: and honor cancellation in your loops with . pitfall: actor reentrancy assumptions state read before an can change after it 03 actors and isolation . re check invariants after every suspension; coalesce duplicate in flight work by caching the . pitfall: blocking the main actor heavy parsing/encoding/image work inside a view's freezes scrolling. push it off main 04 mainactor discipline . pitfall: everywhere detached tasks escape structured concurrency — they don't inherit cancellation, priority, or actor context, and they're easy to leak. use them only for genuinely independent, fire and forget work; prefer structured /task groups otherwise. pattern: bridging callbacks with continuations wrapping an old delegate/closure api into async uses a continuation — call exactly once zero = a leaked forever suspended task; twice = a crash : traps if you misuse — keep it during development. pattern:

app architecture — overview architecture is how you arrange code so an app stays understandable and changeable as it grows. there's no single "right" pattern — there are trade offs. this course covers the spectrum, from the mvc you inherit to the strict unidirectional flows of modern swiftui, so you can choose deliberately instead of by default. the questions architecture answers where does state live? how does the view get it and signal intent? where do side effects network, disk happen? how do you test logic without the ui? how do pieces stay decoupled so one change doesn't ripple everywhere? every pattern is a different set of answers to those five questions. what you'll learn 1. 01 mvc to mvvm — why massive view controller happens and how mvvm splits it. 2. 02 unidirectional data flow — state down, events up; the swiftui native mental model. 3. 03 dependency injection — decoupling from concrete services so code is testable. 4. 04 tca — the composable architecture: reducers, effects, and when it's worth it. 5. 05 modularization — splitting into swift packages for build speed and boundaries. a guiding principle keep logic out of views and side effects at the edges. a view should render state and emit intent; it shouldn't know how to talk to the network or the database. that single rule — pushing i/o to the boundary, keeping a testable core — is what most architectures are rea

mvc → mvvm apple's original pattern is mvc — model, view, controller. it's simple and it's everywhere in uikit, but it has a famous failure mode. mvvm is the most common response, and understanding why it exists matters more than the acronym. classic mvc and its failure mode in theory: the model holds data, the view displays it, the controller mediates. in practice on ios, owns the views and becomes the dumping ground for networking, formatting, navigation, and business logic — the dreaded massive view controller . a 2,000 line controller that can't be tested because everything is tangled with s. the root problem: the controller mixes presentation logic how data becomes display values with view lifecycle and side effects , so none of it can be isolated. mvvm: extract a testable viewmodel mvvm splits the controller in two: viewmodel — owns presentation state and logic. knows nothing about uikit/swiftui. pure, testable. view the controller/swiftui view — observes the viewmodel and renders it; forwards user intent back. the view becomes thin: why this is testable because has no uikit/swiftui imports and takes its by injection, a test creates it with a fake client and asserts the transitions — no ui, no network, milliseconds to run. that's the whole payoff, and it depends on dependency injection. mvvm isn't free pitfalls: viewmodels can themselves bloat split them , bindings can cr

unidirectional data flow unidirectional data flow udf adds a rule mvvm lacks: data moves in one direction . state flows down into the view; the view sends actions up ; actions produce a new state. no two way bindings tangling who changed what. it's the model swiftui itself is built around, and it makes complex screens predictable. the loop state down, events up. the view is a pure function of state; the only way to change state is to send an action through a single place that produces the next state. a minimal udf store every change is an action through → easy to log, replay, and test. you assert "given this state, sending this action yields that state" with no ui. why it scales predictable: there's exactly one way state changes, so bugs are reproducible. debuggable: log every action to get a complete history of what happened. testable: the reducer is a pure function — the easiest thing in the world to test. this is the same precision as swiftui's dependency graph: describe state, and the system re renders only what depends on what changed. side effects pure reducers can't network. udf handles effects by having the reducer return effects async work that run outside and feed their results back as new actions — the model formalized by tca 04 tca . keeping effects at this boundary is the same "i/o at the edges" principle from 00 overview. try it the graph below shows the swiftui v

dependency injection dependency injection di means a component receives the things it needs from outside, instead of creating them itself. it's the single most important technique for testable, decoupled code — and it's mostly just "pass it in," no framework required. the problem with hard coded dependencies this viewmodel is its network call. you can't test it without hitting the real server, can't preview it offline, can't swap implementations. it's welded to . inject an abstraction depend on a protocol , and pass an implementation in: now production injects , tests inject , and swiftui previews inject sample data — same viewmodel, no changes. this is the seam every other course relies on: the api client protocol, the testing fakes in unit testing. forms of injection initializer injection preferred : pass dependencies in . explicit, compile checked, no hidden state. property injection: set after creation. use when init can't have the value yet. environment injection swiftui : put a dependency in the so a subtree can read it — apple's built in di: this is the same environment mechanism from environment as a graph. composition root decide where concrete types are chosen: one place near the app's entry point the "composition root" wires real implementations together, and nothing deep in the app says or . keep the ing of concrete dependencies at the top; everything below receives

the composable architecture tca tca is a popular open source library by point free that takes unidirectional data flow and makes it rigorous, composable, and testable to an exhaustive degree. it's opinionated and has a learning curve — worth knowing whether or not you adopt it, because it crystallizes the ideas. the core types what it buys you composition: features are reducers you can nest and combine; a parent's state contains children's states, and actions route down. big apps stay made of small, isolated features. effects are explicit & controlled: every side effect goes through , and dependencies are injected through a typed dependency system — so tests run with controlled clocks and stubbed services. exhaustive testing: the asserts that every state mutation after every action is exactly what you declared — if anything else changes, the test fails. it's the strictest testability on the platform. the cost tca adds real overhead: a lot of boilerplate, a strong learning curve, and a whole app commitment it works best when everything is tca . for small apps or teams new to it, plain mvvm + the lightweight udf store from 02 unidirectional data flow gets most of the benefit with far less ceremony. when to reach for it large, long lived apps with complex state and many engineers, where the discipline and exhaustive tests pay back the boilerplate. for a solo side project, it's usu

modularization as an app grows, a single target becomes slow to build and easy to tangle. modularization — splitting the app into swift packages with explicit dependencies — restores fast builds, enforced boundaries, and independently testable, previewable features. why split up build speed: changing one feature recompiles only that module, not the whole app. this is the biggest day to day win on a large codebase. enforced boundaries: module a can only use module b if it explicitly depends on it. accidental coupling becomes a compile error instead of a habit. isolation: a feature module can be built, tested, and swiftui previewed on its own, without launching the full app. a typical structure features depend on core and shared, never on each other directly — cross feature communication goes through abstractions or the app shell. swift package manager local spm packages are the standard tool. a declares the module's products, dependencies, and targets: add these as local packages in your xcode project; spm also manages third party dependencies tca, etc. in the same file. keep boundaries clean with protocols modules should depend on abstractions , not each other's concretes — the di principle at module scale. the module depends on a protocol perhaps in a small module , and the app shell injects the concrete implementation. this keeps the dependency graph a tree, not a web. don't

testing & instruments — overview tests let you change code without fear; instruments lets you find out why something is slow instead of guessing. together they're what turn a working prototype into something you can maintain and ship with confidence. what you'll learn 1. 01 xctest and swift testing — the two test frameworks: classic xctest and the modern swift testing. 2. 02 unit testing patterns — what to test, the arrange act assert shape, and testing async code. 3. 03 snapshot and ui tests — verifying rendered ui and end to end flows. 4. 04 instruments — the profiler: time profiler, allocations, core animation, and more. 5. 05 performance and hangs — diagnosing hangs, hitches, and memory growth. the testing pyramid write mostly unit tests fast, pure logic , some integration tests, and a few ui tests for critical flows. inverting this — lots of slow ui tests, few unit tests — gives slow, flaky suites that no one trusts. what makes code testable tests reward the architecture from the previous course: logic in viewmodels/reducers not views , dependencies injected so you can substitute fakes di , and side effects pushed to the edges. if something is hard to test, that's usually a design smell, not a testing problem. how it connects the seams you test were built in networking mockable client and unidirectional flow pure reducers . the performance half pairs with the wwdc sessions

xctest & swift testing ios has two test frameworks now. xctest is the long standing one still used for ui and performance tests . swift testing introduced 2024 is the modern, expressive successor for unit tests. know both — you'll see xctest in every existing codebase and swift testing in new ones. swift testing the modern way macro based, expressive, and parallel by default: marks a function; no prefix needed, and tests can have readable names. is one assertion macro for everything; on failure it shows the actual sub values prints both and . is like but stops the test if it fails use to safely unwrap before continuing . parameterized tests one test, many inputs — no copy paste: each argument runs as its own case and reports independently. suites and setup group tests in a / annotated . a fresh instance is created per test, so stored properties are your isolated setup — no shared mutable state between tests: xctest still essential xctest is required for ui tests and performance tests , and it's what you'll meet in existing projects: the two coexist in the same test bundle, so you can adopt swift testing for new unit tests while keeping xctest for ui/performance. related what to actually test and how: 02 unit testing patterns prev: 00 overview · next: 02 unit testing patterns · hub: 00 overview 🆕 wwdc26 update swift testing picked up four useful additions: issue severity — for s

unit testing patterns knowing the framework is easy; knowing what and how to test is the skill. good unit tests are fast, isolated, deterministic, and read like a specification of behavior. arrange–act–assert every test has the same three beat shape: keep each test focused on one behavior. a test that asserts ten things tells you little when it fails. test behavior, not implementation assert what the code does , not how . tests coupled to internal details break every refactor and discourage improvement. test through the public interface: given inputs/actions, expect outputs/state. the pure reducers from unidirectional flow are ideal here — they're just . inject fakes for dependencies this is where di pays off. replace the real network/clock/store with controllable test doubles: no network, deterministic, runs in milliseconds — and you can test the failure path, which is hard to trigger against a real server. testing async code swift testing supports tests directly — just : for code that emits over time an , collect values with and assert the sequence. inject a controlled clock instead of real so time based logic debounce, retry backoff runs instantly and deterministically. what's worth testing high value: business logic, reducers/viewmodels, parsing/decoding, edge cases empty, nil, huge, malformed , error paths, money/date math. low value: trivial getters, framework behavior do

snapshot & ui tests unit tests verify logic; these two verify the rendered result . snapshot tests catch unintended visual changes; ui tests drive the real app like a user. both are valuable and both are slower and more brittle than unit tests — use them deliberately. snapshot testing a snapshot test renders a view to an image once the "reference" , then fails future runs if the rendering differs. it's the fastest way to catch accidental visual regressions across many states. the first run records the reference image; commit it. later runs diff against it. test multiple states cheaply: light/dark, dynamic type sizes, empty/loaded/error, rtl. each is one line and guards a whole appearance. brittleness is the cost: legitimate design changes require re recording references. keep snapshots for stable components, not rapidly iterating screens. swiftui previews are the informal cousin — with multiple trait variations gives you the same coverage visually during development. ui end to end tests launches the actual app and interacts through the accessibility tree — the closest thing to a real user: keys to non flaky ui tests: find by accessibility identifier , not by visible label or position: . labels change and break tests; identifiers are stable. , never . async ui appears when it appears; fixed sleeps are both slow and flaky. stub the backend. launch the app in a test mode that inje

instruments instruments is xcode's profiler — a suite of tools that record what your app actually does at runtime: where cpu time goes, what's allocating memory, why the ui hitches. the golden rule of performance work: measure first. optimizing without profiling is guessing, and you'll usually fix the wrong thing. how to profile product → profile builds in release configuration optimizations on — critical; debug numbers lie and launches instruments. pick a template, record while you reproduce the problem, then read the trace. the instruments you'll use most time profiler — samples the call stack 1000×/sec to show where cpu time is spent. the "heaviest stack trace" tells you the hot path. look for your code high in the tree, and for unexpected main thread work. allocations — every allocation and what's still alive. use it to find memory growth and large transient allocations e.g. full size images you forgot to downsample, images & bitmaps . leaks — flags memory that's unreachable but not freed, usually a retain cycle arc . pair with the memory graph debugger to see the cycle. core animation / animation hitches — surfaces dropped frames, off screen rendering, and blended layers; the tool for jank in scrolling and animation drawing performance . swiftui — shows view re evaluations: which views recompute, how often, and why. the profiler for "my swiftui is slow." reading a time pro

performance & hangs users don't read your code — they feel its performance. three distinct problems show up as "the app feels bad": hangs the ui freezes , hitches a frame is dropped mid animation , and memory growth it gets slow then gets killed . each has a different cause and a different fix. hangs: the main thread is blocked a hang is the main thread doing synchronous work so nothing can respond. anything over 250ms is perceptible; over a few seconds the watchdog may kill the app. causes and fixes: synchronous i/o on main disk reads, a big json decode, an unindexed core data fetch → move it off the main actor @mainactor discipline . heavy layout/draw on main → simplify, cache, or rasterize drawing performance . a lock or waiting on something slow while holding up the ui. apple's eliminate hangs session is the definitive treatment; the time profiler shows you exactly which main thread call is blocking. hitches: a missed frame the display refreshes every 16.7ms or 8.3ms at 120hz promotion . if a frame's work overruns that budget, the frame is dropped — a hitch — visible as a stutter in a scroll or animation. causes: commit phase too long: too many layers, off screen passes un pathed shadows, + masking , overdraw. main thread work during the animation even brief steals from the frame budget. use the animation hitches instrument; it attributes each hitch to the commit or render

app intents & siri app intents is the framework that exposes your app's actions to the system . one swift definition becomes a siri command, a shortcuts action, a spotlight result, and an interactive widget button — all at once. it's one of the highest leverage things you can add, because it multiplies where your features can be invoked. an intent is a verb is the action; s are the inputs the system can prompt for. because the intent is a serializable value, the system can run it from anywhere — even when your app isn't open the same property that makes interactive widgets work . entities and queries to act on your data "call my plumber", "open the acme project" , model it as an with a query so the system can resolve names to objects: now an intent can take a parameter and siri/shortcuts will let the user pick or speak one. shortcuts and siri phrases expose intents to siri and the shortcuts app with , including spoken phrases: these appear automatically in spotlight and shortcuts, and siri understands the phrases — no separate siri code. why this is worth it app intents is a small amount of code for an outsized payoff: your features become os level capabilities the user can automate, voice trigger, and pin. it's also future facing — it's how features surface in newer system experiences. define your key actions as intents and you get reach you'd otherwise never build. related sa

accessibility accessibility a11y makes your app usable by people with vision, motor, hearing, or cognitive differences. it's the right thing to do, it's expected on apple platforms, and — a nice side effect — accessible ui is more testable and often better designed for everyone. swiftui gives you a lot for free; the rest is small, deliberate additions. voiceover: the screen reader voiceover reads the ui aloud and lets users navigate by swiping. standard swiftui controls are already accessible; your job is to make custom views and decorative compositions read sensibly: mark purely decorative images so voiceover skips noise. dynamic type users can scale text system wide, up to huge accessibility sizes. use semantic font styles , which scale automatically — never hard coded point sizes: then make sure layouts survive large type: prefer flexible layouts, allow wrapping, and test at the largest accessibility size. switch hstacks to vstacks at huge sizes with . contrast and color don't rely on color alone to convey meaning color blind users — pair it with text or an icon. meet contrast ratios, and respect increase contrast and reduce transparency . use semantic colors , , asset colors with dark variants so the system adapts them. reduce motion some users get motion sickness from large animations. honor their setting and swap big movement for a cross fade: this is exactly the courtesy

swift package manager swift package manager spm is the built in tool for adding third party dependencies and for splitting your own code into reusable modules. it replaced cocoapods/carthage for most projects and is integrated directly into xcode. adding a dependency in xcode: file → add package dependencies , paste a repo url, choose a version rule. or in a : version rules matter spm uses semantic versioning. choose how much you trust upstream: the resolved versions are recorded in — commit it so everyone and ci builds the exact same dependency versions. authoring your own package a package is a folder with a manifest describing products what it exposes , targets compilation units , and their dependencies — the structure behind modularization: reference local packages by path to build a multi module app from sibling folders. resources and access control bundle assets, json, or files with / and load them via . remember that types are by default — anything a consumer of your package must use needs to be or . forgetting is the most common "why can't i import this?" confusion. practical tips keep dependency count low; each one is code you don't control, plus build time and a supply chain surface. prefer well maintained packages with real release tags over . if xcode's package cache misbehaves, file → packages → reset package caches fixes most resolution gremlins. related what you

code signing code signing is the part of ios development everyone dreads, usually because it's treated as magic. it isn't — it's a small set of pieces that together prove who built an app and what it's allowed to do. understand the pieces and the dread goes away. the four pieces a signed build = your binary + a provisioning profile that says "this certificate is allowed to run this bundle id with these entitlements on these devices ." automatic signing use this for most apps, xcode's automatically manage signing is the right choice: check the box, pick your team, and xcode creates and renews certificates and profiles for you. add a capability push, app groups, sign in with apple in that same tab and xcode updates the app id's entitlements and regenerates the profile automatically. this is the 90% path. when you manage it manually ci servers, enterprise distribution, and teams with strict control sometimes manage signing by hand: create the app id and capabilities in the developer portal, generate a distribution certificate, create a profile, and download both. tools like fastlane match store a shared set of certificates/profiles in a git repo so every machine and ci runner signs identically — the usual fix for "works on my mac, fails on the build server." distribution flavors development — run on registered devices during dev. app store — upload to app store connect for testfli

xcode cloud & the app store the last mile: getting a signed build tested by real users and onto the app store, ideally automatically. this lesson covers continuous integration with xcode cloud, testflight, and the app store connect release flow. continuous integration ci runs your tests on every push and produces builds without anyone manually archiving. xcode cloud is apple's built in option no servers to manage, integrated with app store connect : alternatives — github actions, fastlane, bitrise — do the same with more control and often lower cost at scale; many teams use fastlane lanes invoked from github actions. whatever the tool, the pipeline is the same: build → test → sign → upload . the build → release pipeline testflight testflight is how you get real world testing before release. internal testers up to 100 team members get builds immediately; external testers up to 10,000, via public link get them after a lightweight beta review. builds expire after 90 days. use it to catch device specific bugs, gather feedback, and validate analytics before going live. app store submission essentials a submission needs: an app icon and screenshots for required device sizes, a description and keywords, a privacy policy url, and the privacy nutrition label declare what data you collect and why plus the privacy manifest for the app and sdks. incomplete or inaccurate privacy info is a c

origami studio origami studio is meta's free prototyping tool for designing real, physics based interactions — the kind that ship in facebook, instagram, whatsapp, and quest. where figma prototyping is about linking screens, origami is about behavior : how something moves, responds to a finger, and feels. it's the natural companion to your interaction math and fluid interface work — the same ideas springs, gesture as function, velocity made visual and hands on. origami is mac only , completely free with no subscriptions or feature limits, and pairs with origami live , a free ios app that runs your prototype on a real device so you can feel the interaction in your hand. the three areas of the interface origami's window has three panels, and almost everything you do lives in one of them: you design appearance in layers and behavior in the patch editor. the viewer is always live, which is what makes origami feel immediate. patches: visual programming a patch is a node with inputs left and outputs right . you wire an output of one patch into the input of another to build logic — no text code. a simple interaction reads like a sentence drawn left to right: that graph means: "when the layer is tapped, flip a switch, and spring the layer's scale to a new value." the whole tool is composing these little data flow graphs — exactly the model the behavior as a function mindset from modeli
